title: Emergent Road Rules In Multi-Agent Driving Environments

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Summary: The output of the work is an MDP model that is capable of encoding complex traffic rules including traffic signals, lanes, right of way FIFO etc. Various different traffic environments such as intersections, highways, nuScenes are considered.

The MDP that results from this work is very useful for future research. Currently, there are very few simulators that encode complex traffic rules or provide the flexibility to perform research. Therefore, the possibility of new simulators resulting from this MDP is exciting and useful to encourage and promote research in autonomous driving, especially in dense and heterogeneous environments.

I am glad that the authors have provided the source code **with extensive documentation and instructions** on how to run the code and reproduce the results. 

Unless there is some major flaw with this paper (that I may have missed), there is no reason to reject this paper.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Review:

Summary:
The paper presents a carefully designed multi-agent Markov Decision Process (MDP) that gives rise to a variety of human-like “road rules” in simulated driving scenarios. By training agents with noisy LiDAR observations and a reward that encourages reaching destinations quickly without collisions, the authors empirically demonstrate the emergence of seven social driving behaviors—ranging from obeying traffic signals and following lanes to respecting right-of-way and maintaining safety distances. Experiments are conducted across multiple environments, including synthetic four-way intersections, highway tracks, and real-world road patches drawn from the nuScenes dataset. The result is an MDP specification, released alongside well-documented code, which offers a flexible benchmark for future research in autonomous driving and multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL).

Strengths:
1. Novel MDP Design: The paper’s main technical contribution is the identification of two critical ingredients—perception noise and agent spatial density—that lead to emergent road rules when agents optimize the utilitarian traffic-flow objective. By systematically ablating lidar noise (0–75% ray dropout) and varying the number of agents (from single-agent up to 80 agents), the authors show, for example, that increased noise makes agents more reliant on traffic lights (Figure 2–3) and that higher agent density yields clear lane formation (Figure 4–5).

2. Rich Set of Emergent Behaviors: Unlike earlier work that hard-codes rules or focuses on imitation learning, this approach yields the spontaneous appearance of standard traffic norms, such as FIFO-based right-of-way at nuScenes intersections (85.3% compliance, Figure 7), fast-lane segregation on highways (Figure 10–11), safety-distance maintenance (98.45% adherence, Figure 12), and pedestrian-aware deceleration at crosswalks (Figure 13). This breadth underscores the MDP’s expressiveness.

3. Flexible Policy Parameterizations: The paper introduces both a “fixed track” policy model (optimizing acceleration on pre-defined trajectories) and a more expressive “spline” model (predicting clothoid splines plus velocity profiles). This design choice allows exploration of lane discovery and more complex maneuvers within the same overall framework, as detailed in Section 4.4 and Algorithm 1.

4. Centralized Training, Decentralized Execution: The use of a centralized critic with permutation-invariant pooling of latent vectors (mean over agents) stabilizes training in non-stationary, variable-population settings, and demonstrates effective scaling to dozens of agents.

5. Comprehensive Evaluation and Open Source: The authors test their approach in diverse map topologies—including synthetic intersections, real nuScenes scenes (Figure 8), and highway tracks—and provide both qualitative visualizations and quantitative metrics. The accompanying code repository contains extensive documentation, simulation scripts, and pre-trained models, enabling reproducibility and facilitating community adoption.

Detailed Comments:
– Section 5’s breakdown of emergent behaviors is particularly insightful. The traffic-signal experiment (5.1) not only shows agents learn to stop on red lights but also quantitatively illustrates how noisy perception leads to more conservative braking well before the intersection (Figure 2).  
– In Section 5.2, the spline-based approach elegantly uncovers discrete lanes: under high noise levels, the distribution of normalized lane positions exhibits sharp bimodal peaks (Figure 4), whereas with perfect perception agents converge to riskier, overlapping trajectories.  
– The communication experiment (5.4) further highlights the model’s richness: a one-bit signaling channel yields increased speaker consistency and higher Pearson correlation between message and agent heading as perception degrades (Figure 9), indicating robust emergent signaling protocols.  
– The safety-distance analysis in Section 5.6 directly parallels human driving statistics (Figure 14), showing the MDP captures key behavioral norms, albeit with slightly higher violation rates, which suggests avenues for further refinement.

Suggestions for Improvement:
– While the paper focuses on sparse LiDAR and high-level state features, future work could integrate richer sensor modalities (e.g., camera frames) to examine if similar rules emerge in high-dimensional observation spaces.  
– The current reward does not explicitly penalize lane changes, which inhibits testing of dynamic lane-switching behavior. Extending the action space to include lateral control could be a next step.  
– A brief user study or more direct comparison against human driving data in nuScenes (beyond safety distance) would strengthen empirical alignment with real-world behavior.

Overall, this paper delivers a well-motivated MDP design, compelling empirical evidence of emergent traffic norms, and a practical codebase. It opens promising avenues for MARL research in realistic driving contexts and sets a strong foundation for future simulator development.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper investigate how to design simulation environments so the the agent trained with them can master social rules.
Cons:
1. The paper is well written and easy to read and understand. Thanks!
2. The experiments are solid and well defined. 

My major concern of this paper are:
1. The authors seems to only considered the noise of sensors and the number of agents, and these two factors happened to induce social behaviors like following lanes and stopping at traffic signals. In other words, I am not quite convinced that with all vehicles being automated, sensor noise will cause them to formulate rules that what human drivers follow today. Since human driving interactions are complex, I do not think that sensor noise would be enough to induce them. 
2. I would be happy to see how this configuration of simulation environment compares with reward guided social behaviors. For example, we can design a reward to encourage agents follow right of way. I think this way might be more direct and powerful. 
2. The number of agents seems to be too small and may affect the formulation of road social rules.
3. It seems like the agents did not take traffic signal status as input for the action selection, then how did they formulate the signal control rules. 
4. Figures 2 and 3 needs better explanations for readers to understand. 

Overall, I think this paper needs better formulation of the logics and also a deep investigation of how the simulation variations lead to those behaviors.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 2: The reviewer is willing to defend the evaluation, but it is quite likely that the reviewer did not understand central parts of the paper
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The paper proposes to learn traffic rules (e.g. traffic lights, speed limits) via multi-agent RL (MARL) from observations rather than hardcoding the rules into the algorithms. To this end, the authors claims contributions in:

-- Defining a multi-agent driving environment, where each agent has incomplete observation (noisy LiDAR), and is rewarded for reaching a destination quickly without colliding. Experiments show that road rules can be learned.

-- Ablation on the choice of the MDP, and insights that perception noise and spatial density of agents are important to successfully learn the environment.

-- Authors promise to release a suite of 2D driving environments for future MARL research in self-driving.

I must admit that I am mainly a computer vision person, and I only have limited experience with RL or MARL. However, I hope that my assessment below is still better than an educated guess. I apologize in advance if there is any obvious misunderstanding.

Strengths:

-- Novelty in problem statement and at a high level: The problem statement of learning hard traffic rules via observing logs seems new. The application of MARL to the problem is new too AFAIK. And as the authors stated, the majority of multi-agent behavior works have been using imitation learning. (Maybe consider citing [1] line of work as well, which deals with multi-agent imitation learning). The proposed PPO-based method seems a good alternative.

-- Extensive experiments: There are 7 small but specific tasks such as "Traffic lights", "Emergence of lanes", etc., where the authors provided evidences to the claims that perception noise and spatial density of agents are crucial for the method's success.

[1]: Multi-Agent Generative Adversarial Imitation Learning

Weaknesses:

-- No comparison to prior work: The authors acknowledged imitation learning-based (IL) methods but did any quantitative comparison for them. I don't see any evidence why solving the proposed problem using MARL is better than IL. So why are we doing MARL over IL?

- Usefulness: for the traffic light use case, the handling of red / green lights is essentially learned by NOT driving into other cars, which obey the traffic rules. With other words, if there are no other agents to demonstrate how to behave, the agent will always prefer to run over red. This raises the question of the usefulness of the system.

-- Lack of novelty in the method itself: The paper seems to be using an off-the-shelf PPO. The centralized critic, single-step PPO, and bi-level PPO do not provide sufficient novelty in terms of methods. The centralized critic can be seen as a straightforward extension of PPO for the self-driving application. The bi-level extension is a straightforward way to optimize two objectives at the same time, which is quite common even in the ages of convex optimization (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biconvex_optimization). I think that there __is__ novelty in the method, but I am not sure if it's enough for this venue.

- Dataset: NuScenes dataset is a perception dataset, and probably does not contain many interesting interactive scenarios. Still, the method does not seem to perform very well in the experiments. E.g., according to Fig 2., many cars are still accelerating despite the red light...

Details:

- All axes names and titles in all diagrams are way too small. No way people can decipher them if printed on paper.

- No legends in the diagrams. Barely any caption. Please make diagrams self-contained if possible.

Conclusion:

This works provides an interesting new problem statement and explores the area of using MARL for autonomous driving. My main concerns regarding this paper are the lack of comparison to prior works in the experiments, and the work provides any usefulness and improvements over current autonomous systems, which are mostly based on imitation learning.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper tackles the novel problem of learning human-like road rules through multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) rather than hard-coding them. Specifically, the authors propose a Dec-POMDP framework in which each autonomous driving agent is equipped with a noisy 2D LiDAR sensor, limited-range perception (50 m), and is rewarded for reaching a randomly sampled destination as quickly as possible without collisions. They train the agents with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) using a centralized critic during training and decentralized execution, and in one set of experiments employ a bilevel optimization to learn both spatial “spline” subpolicies (lane structure) and acceleration subpolicies. Through qualitative rollouts and quantitative analyses, the paper demonstrates the emergence of seven distinct social driving behaviors (traffic signal compliance, lane following, right-of-way adherence, inter-agent communication, fast lanes on highways, minimum safety distances, and pedestrian crosswalk yielding), and performs ablations over perception noise levels (0%–75% LiDAR dropout) and spatial agent density (1 to 80 vehicles). Finally, the authors release their 2D driving environments to the community.

Strengths  
1. Novel problem statement.  The idea of inducing traffic rules as emergent behaviors in MARL agents—rather than encoding them explicitly—is, to my knowledge, new. While many prior self-driving efforts rely on imitation learning (Bojarski et al., 2016; Zeng et al., 2019; Bansal et al., 2018), this work demonstrates that a pure reinforcement learning approach, with the right environment design, can recover conventions such as stopping at red lights or forming fast lanes.  
2. Clear MDP design insights.  The authors systematically isolate the two most important ingredients for robust emergence: (i) perception noise, which forces agents to adopt safety margins and constructive heuristics (e.g., lanes to cope with limited LiDAR), and (ii) spatial density, which creates mutual interaction pressure (e.g., vehicles must coordinate to cross intersections). These insights are well supported by Figures 2–5.  
3. Extensive empirical suite.  The paper reports seven focused experiments across synthetic 4-way intersections, highway tracks, and real-world nuScenes road patches. Each behavior is examined under varying LiDAR noise and agent counts, and supplemented with both box plots (action vs. distance to intersection) and spatial histograms (lane position heatmaps).  
4. Open-source release.  By providing their 2D driving MDP environments (https://github.com/fidler-lab/social-driving), the authors facilitate reproducibility and lower the barrier for future MARL research in self-driving.

Weaknesses  
1. No direct comparison to imitation learning methods.  Although the authors rightly point out that most prior research on emergent traffic behaviors uses imitation learning, the paper contains no quantitative baseline against a state-of-the-art multi-agent generative adversarial imitation learning approach (e.g., Multi-Agent GAIL). Without showing, for example, that MARL yields better generalization under novel traffic patterns or less reliance on expert trajectories, it is hard to assess the practical gains of learning road rules via reinforcement learning instead of imitation.  
2. Questionable real-world usefulness.  The traffic light experiment in Section 5.1 demonstrates obedience to red lights only when other vehicles are present. In single-agent rollouts (Figure 3, top-left), red-light compliance vanishes altogether. This suggests that agents learn traffic signal rules indirectly—by avoiding collisions with other compliant agents—rather than developing an intrinsic concept of “red means stop.” In safety-critical applications, relying solely on emergent social pressure may be insufficient when fractionally mixed with human drivers or malfunctioning agents.  
3. Limited methodological novelty.  The core learning algorithm is standard PPO with a centralized critic plus fairly straightforward extensions—a single-step PPO variant for spline selection and a bilevel alternating optimization (Algorithm 1) for subpolicy learning. While these engineering choices are sensible, they do not constitute substantial algorithmic contributions. In particular, the bilevel PPO resembles classical block-coordinate updates (cf. bi-convex optimization), and the centralized critic follows directly from prior MARL work (e.g., MADDPG, COMA).  
4. Dataset/environment constraints.  The use of nuScenes intersections and straight highway tracks provides realistic map layouts but may lack rich interactive scenarios (e.g., complex merges, roundabouts, pedestrian clusters). Indeed, performance on real-world patches shows non-negligible collision rates (Figure 7 reports ~8.9% crash rate even after convergence). It remains unclear how well policies trained in the 2D MDP would transfer to richer 3D simulators (e.g., CARLA) or incorporate full sensor modalities (camera, radar).  
5. Presentation issues.  Many figures (e.g., Figures 2, 4, 12, 13) have small axis labels and no legends explaining color codes or bin definitions. The captions are terse, and readers must refer back to the text to decode which box corresponds to which LiDAR noise level or agent density. Self-contained plots with clear legends would greatly improve readability.

Detailed Comments  
- Section 3 defines the Dec-POMDP with reward components carefully bounded between 0 and 1. While high-level rewards (destination +1, collision −1, smoothness penalty, progress penalty) reduce reward engineering, the combination of four terms might still bias learning; an ablation on removing the longitudinal distance penalty, for instance, would clarify its necessity.  
- In Section 4.1, the Fixed Track vs. Spline models are well motivated, but the Fixed Track setting precludes any lateral deviation, making it essentially a 1D acceleration task. The spline model’s use of Centripetal Catmull-Rom splines is a solid choice, yet prevents true lane-change maneuvers by construction. A future extension could allow agents to directly output 2D heading angles or lateral accelerations.  
- The emergent communication experiment (Sec 5.4) is interesting: by opening a discrete one-bit “turn signal” channel, agents improve safety under high LiDAR noise. The Speaker Consistency metric and Pearson correlation between heading and message (Figure 9) rise from ~0.04 to ~0.13 as perception worsens, showing that communication is adaptively used to compensate for uncertain sensors.  
- The fast lane highway result (Sec 5.5 and Figures 10, 11) neatly captures how acceleration-rated agents segregate into different lanes: higher-rated vehicles adopt the right lanes while slower ones keep left. However, the metric is purely spatial; it would be useful to report average throughput (e.g., number of vehicles passing per unit time) to quantify efficiency gains.  
- In Sec 5.7, the crosswalk experiment with 10 pedestrians tests pedestrian yielding. Figure 13’s KDE plots show most agents maintain >6 m distance—a reasonable “speed-matching” stopping distance—but no statistics on actual collisions or near-misses are given. A quantitative collision rate vs. baseline would strengthen this section.

Conclusion  
This paper presents an insightful exploration of how traffic rules and social driving behaviors can emerge from pure MARL in 2D driving environments with noisy perception and multi-agent interactions. The problem statement and empirical findings are novel and well documented. However, the absence of comparisons to imitation learning baselines, limited methodological innovation beyond standard PPO variants, and unclear real-world applicability temper the paper’s impact. Given these considerations, I believe the work merits recognition for its exploratory contribution but falls marginally below the bar for acceptance.

reviews.confidence: 2: The reviewer is willing to defend the evaluation, but it is quite likely that the reviewer did not understand central parts of the paper
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Summary:
This paper proposes a bilevel MARL method that can learn road rules and conventions implicitly without hard coding. It is quite interesting to see a simple and straightforward idea that is effective in this task setting. However, this paper needs to be further polished in terms of its delivery, completeness, and evaluation.

Methodology:
How is the noise introduced to the mimicked LiDAR perception results?
In Spline Model, does the trajectory shape only depends on the initial state without considering the afterward interaction?
The clarity and completeness of writing could be significantly improved.
The equations, terms, and the algorithm in Section 4 need sufficient explanations. E.g., what are vf^{target}_t, H, K_1, K_2, N? What is the complete reward list?

Experiments:
It seems the route is relatively short, and the driving scenarios used for different tasks are distinct. I was wondering whether the generalization of the proposed method and the learned rules could be validated.

No baselines have been evaluated or compared. Which experimental results are or the fixed track model? What are the differences between the two models in the results? What are the reward values during the training phase?

The captions and axis labels are difficult to read.
What do the colors mean in Fig. 2?


====
My rating has been updated after the rebuttal.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review:

Summary:  
This paper proposes a bilevel multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework in which common road rules and driving conventions—such as obeying traffic lights, following lanes, and respecting right of way—emerge organically rather than being hard-coded into the agents’ policies. The core idea of training agents to maximize traffic flow under noisy perception and modest reward engineering is appealing: it suggests a scalable alternative to manual rule specification. The authors validate their approach on several synthetic 2D environments (four‐way intersections, highway tracks) and real-world road patches from the nuScenes dataset, and they demonstrate seven distinct social driving behaviors emerging from the same reward structure. However, despite these promising results, the manuscript requires significant improvements in delivery, completeness of methodological exposition, and depth of experimental evaluation before it can be considered for acceptance.

Methodology:

• Noise in LiDAR Perception  
  The paper mentions in Section 3 that “x% lidar noise” is introduced by randomly dropping a fraction of LiDAR rays at each timestep (citing Manivasagam et al., 2020). While this is a reasonable mechanism, the precise sampling distribution (uniform or otherwise), the dependence on environment complexity, and whether noise is applied independently across timesteps or correlated over time are not fully specified. Clarifying whether the dropped rays are reassigned a zero reading or perturbed by Gaussian noise, and including a short pseudocode snippet or explicit equation for this noise model, would greatly enhance reproducibility.

• Spline Model and Interaction Effects  
  In Section 4.1, the Spline Model is described as producing a clothoid-based spatial path from the agent’s initial local observation. It is unclear, however, whether this spline subpolicy can adjust the predicted path mid-trajectory in response to interactions with other agents, or if the shape is entirely fixed at rollout start. Given that dynamic obstacle avoidance and lane switching are critical for realistic driving, the authors should clarify whether the spline is re-computed at every decision step based on updated observations or remains static after its initial sampling.

• Clarity and Completeness of Writing  
  Throughout Sections 3 and 4, the presentation could benefit from more consistent notation and clearer prose. For example, the transition from the Dec-POMDP formalism to the specific PPO objectives is abrupt; a brief roadmap paragraph at the start of Section 4 would help readers follow the sequence of subpolicy optimizations. Additionally, the reward components (destination reward, collision penalty, action‐smoothness regularizer, normalized distance penalty) are introduced in prose, but a consolidated table listing each term, its mathematical form, and its normalization bounds would eliminate potential ambiguities.

• Explanation of Algorithmic Terms  
  Section 4’s optimization algorithm (Algorithm 1) refers to quantities such as vf_target, H(s, π), K₁, K₂, and N without definition. For instance, vf_target presumably denotes a target value estimate used in the value‐function update, and H(s, π) is the policy entropy, but neither is formally presented. Similarly, the integers K₁ and K₂ (number of minibatches for spline and acceleration subpolicies) and N (total training iterations) should be given typical values or ranges in an experiment setup paragraph. Providing these details will allow readers to fully grasp the bilevel PPO procedure.

Experiments:

• Generalization Beyond Short Routes  
  The driving tasks considered involve relatively short routes within a single intersection or a short highway segment. It remains an open question whether the learned rules transfer to longer, more complex maps, such as city grids with multiple intersections or highway networks with on/off-ramps. An experiment testing zero-shot generalization of a trained policy on an unseen map would strengthen the claim of emergent, robust road rules.

• Lack of Baseline Comparisons  
  No existing methods or simple ablations are provided as baselines. For instance, how does performance compare to (a) a centralized planner that is hard-coded with standard traffic rules, (b) agents trained without noisy perception, or (c) a single-agent RL approach on the same environment? Furthermore, it is unclear which set of plots (e.g., Figures 2 and 3) correspond to the fixed track model versus the spline model. Reporting performance metrics (collision rate, average travel time, emergence statistics) side-by-side for both policy parameterizations would clarify their relative merits.

• Training Dynamics and Reward Curves  
  The manuscript reports final statistics (e.g., 85.25±8.9% right‐of‐way adherence), but omits learning curves for reward components and safety metrics during training. Plotting the average episodic reward, collision penalty, and distance‐to‐goal penalty over the course of learning would provide insights into convergence speed, stability, and potential overfitting to specific traffic densities.

• Figure Readability and Annotation  
  Several figures (particularly Figures 2, 3, and 4) have small axis labels and rely on color coding without a clear legend. In Figure 2, for example, the box plots are color‐shaded supposedly to indicate traffic‐signal states, but the caption does not specify which color maps to red, yellow, or green signals. Enlarging font sizes, adding explicit legends, and ensuring all axes are fully labeled (distance in meters, acceleration in m/s², noise percentage) will greatly improve clarity.

• Interpretation of Emergent Behaviors Across Models  
  While the paper qualitatively describes seven behaviors (signal obedience, lane following, right of way, communication, fast lanes, safety distance, pedestrian avoidance), it does not quantify how each behavior’s emergence depends on perception noise versus agent density. A heat‐map or sensitivity analysis showing the degree of lane discipline or signal compliance across combinations of LiDAR noise (0–100%) and agent density (4–80 vehicles) would give a more nuanced view of which environmental factors most strongly drive specific rules.

Decision and Rating:  
My evaluation, after considering the rebuttal and additional clarifications, remains unchanged. The idea of emergent road rules via MARL under noisy perception is compelling and addresses an important problem in autonomous driving, but the manuscript falls short in methodological detail, baseline comparisons, and evaluation breadth. For these reasons, I rate the paper as 5: Marginally below acceptance threshold.

title: not-MIWAE: Deep Generative Modelling with Missing not at Random Data

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Overall I very much enjoyed reading this paper! The manuscript is very well written, the related work section is sound, the motivation is clear, the methodology is well formalized and the experimental validation is strong in some aspects. 

The formalisation of the missingness process is very intuitive. Also the literature referenced provides one of the most comprehensive overviews on the topic in the statistics community, on the deep learning side it seems that there is a lot of research covered on the side of deep learning latent variable models based on variational auto encoders (VAEs), but the complementary work on Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) appears to be less well covered. I’m myself more familiar with the VAE approach, and the authors correctly mention the implicit assumptions of GAN approaches, but as GANs offer an intuitive parametric and flexible way of modelling the missingness mask, I guess it would be helpful to see them in the comparison. 

In the first paragraph on page 5 the authors mention that the approach with the reparametrization only works if the data is continuous. I might be missing something, but rather than using a sampling based approach as in Mohamed et al, referenced by the authors, maybe it would be an option to use something like the Gumbel Softmax? In the experimental section the authors use that approach for the recommender system data, with limited success, it seems, compared to a plain gaussian likelihood. But conceptually it would be good to comment on why that’s not possible? 

My main concerns with this work is the experimental validation. The experimental settings explored are UCI data sets, image data and recommendation systems. It’s great that the authors provide such a comprehensive and heterogenous experimental validation in terms of data sets. What I found a bit limiting is that the not-missing-at-random process was so simple and restricted, and that the missingness ratio was not explored systematically. Many studies on imputation use experimental validation that explores missingness ratios between 0 and 100% of the values and in particular the MAR and MNAR settings are explored by, e.g. sampling a random quantile of a feature to condition the missingness on. This is very simple to implement but would allow for a much more realistic account of missingness structure. Especially for a study like this, which makes a presumably important and strong contribution to the field of missing data imputation I would recommend to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach by a more realistic experimental setting. 

Another recommendation would be to highlight the advantage of the proposed approach with more synthetic data experiments as in figure 1b, maybe one linear and one non-linear data manifold. That would allow to control for more parameters like the rank of the data and the noise, their covariance structure (strength and independence of features and noise, respectively). But that’s not really necessary I think, the authors did a great job with figure 1b!

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Review:
Overall I very much enjoyed reading this paper! The manuscript is very well written, the related work section is sound, the motivation is clear, the methodology is well formalized and the experimental validation is strong in some aspects. 

The formalisation of the missingness process is very intuitive. Also the literature referenced provides one of the most comprehensive overviews on the topic in the statistics community, on the deep learning side it seems that there is a lot of research covered on the side of deep learning latent variable models based on variational auto encoders (VAEs), but the complementary work on Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) appears to be less well covered. I’m myself more familiar with the VAE approach, and the authors correctly mention the implicit assumptions of GAN approaches, but as GANs offer an intuitive parametric and flexible way of modelling the missingness mask, I guess it would be helpful to see them in the comparison. 

In the first paragraph on page 5 the authors mention that the approach with the reparametrization only works if the data is continuous. I might be missing something, but rather than using a sampling based approach as in Mohamed et al, referenced by the authors, maybe it would be an option to use something like the Gumbel Softmax? reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The paper introduces a deep latent variable model (DLVM) for missing data problems where the missing mechanism is missing not at random (MNAR) and therefore cannot be ignored. It presents an approach for fitting the model based on importance-weighted variational inference and reparameterization trick, and demonstrates the application of the proposed method in simulated and real data sets.

Pros:

-The paper addresses an important problem of dealing with MNAR data, by introducing a DLVM model that allows for incorporating prior information about the type of missingness (for example, self centoring) into the model. This extends the applicability of DLVM models to a wider class of practical problems.

-The paper is clearly written.

-The experimental results demonstrate the trade-off between data model flexibility and missing model flexibility.


Cons:

-The proposed approach is a relatively straightforward extension of the existing work (MIWAE, Mattei & Frellsen, 2019), using somewhat standard VAE techniques.

-I’m not sure the experimental results really demonstrated the advantages of the proposed methods against existing ones, particularly in the settings of no prior knowledge about the type of missingness mechanism. Though seeing examples of the trade-off between data model flexibility and missing model flexibility is nice.

Overall, I think the results in the paper should be useful in a number of applications and the paper has enough contributions to merit publication.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper tackles the challenging problem of performing likelihood‐based inference and imputation when data are missing not at random (MNAR), i.e., when the missingness mechanism itself depends on the unobserved values. Building on the recent Missing‐data Importance‐Weighted Autoencoder (MIWAE) by Mattei & Frellsen (2019), the authors propose the not‐MIWAE: a deep latent variable model (DLVM) that jointly models the data generating process pθ(x|z) and the missingness mechanism pφ(s|x). Their key technical contribution is to derive a single importance‐weighted variational bound on the joint likelihood of observed features and the missingness mask, which admits stochastic gradient estimation via reparameterization both in latent space z and in the data space xm. Empirically, the paper demonstrates on a PPCA‐style MNAR toy example, UCI‐benchmark real data, a clipping‐in‐SVHN image experiment, and a selection‐bias scenario in the Yahoo! R3 recommendation dataset that explicitly modelling pφ(s|x) can reduce bias and improve imputation and held‐out log-likelihood.

Strengths  
• Important problem addressed.  MNAR data arise in many real‐world domains (e.g. censoring in medical records, selection bias in recommender systems), yet most deep learning approaches assume MAR or MCAR. By providing a general recipe to incorporate prior knowledge about missingness—such as self‐masking via logistic regression or more elaborate missing models—the not‐MIWAE extends the applicability of DLVMs to genuinely non‐ignorable missing data.  
• Clear model formulation.  The paper’s section 2.1 “PPCA example” (Fig. 1b) neatly illustrates how ignoring MNAR (standard PPCA) leads to biased mean and principal directions, whereas the not‐MIWAE PPCA recovers the correct contour under a simple self‐censoring mechanism. This concrete graphical‐model depiction (Fig. 1a) helps readers quickly grasp the joint latent‐data‐mask generative process.  
• Easy integration of prior information.  The authors show that one can choose pφ(s|x) as a Bernoulli with feature‐wise probabilities πφ,j(x), implemented either “agnostically” (a single dense layer mapping the decoder output to mask logits) or with structured self‐masking logistic regression (known sign constraints on weights), as detailed in Section 3.1 and Appendix C. This flexibility is a useful design pattern for practitioners who have domain knowledge about how data become missing.  
• Solid inference strategy.  By extending the IWAE bound, the authors derive LK(θ, φ, γ) in Equations (7)–(8) and show (via Theorem 3 from Domke & Sheldon, 2018) that this lower‐bound converges monotonically to the true joint data‐mask likelihood (Equation 9). The reparameterization‐based gradients for both qγ(z|xo) and pθ(xm|z) are implemented in TensorFlow Probability, making the method accessible to applied researchers.  
• Empirical trade‐off insights.  The UCI experiments (Table 1, Figures 5–7 in Appendix E) illustrate how increasing model flexibility in pθ(x|z) demands correspondingly stronger inductive biases in pφ(s|x). In the limit, a highly flexible decoder (deep convolutional nets on SVHN) requires us to supply a near‐correct missing model (Equation 11) to achieve low RMSE (0.0729 vs 0.1730 for MIWAE). Conversely, a simple PPCA‐style data model tolerates a more agnostic missing model. These detailed ablations give practical guidance on balancing data‐model and missing‐model complexity.  
• Strong real‐world demonstration.  On the Yahoo! R3 recommendation dataset (Table 3), the not‐MIWAE outperforms both standard MAR approaches (e.g. PMF, AutoRec, Gaussian‐VAE) and recent debiasing methods based on inverse propensity scoring (MF‐IPS) and doubly robust estimators (MF‐DR‐JL, NFM‐DR‐JL). Notably, unlike these propensity‐based methods, not‐MIWAE requires no held‐out MCAR data to learn pφ(s|x), yet still achieves state‐of‐the‐art imputation MSE of 0.939 (Gaussian) and 1.293 (categorical).  

Weaknesses  
• Limited novelty beyond MIWAE.  The not‐MIWAE framework can be viewed as a straightforward extension of Mattei & Frellsen’s MIWAE, with the only structural addition being an explicit missing‐mask network pφ(s|x) inserted into the importance‐weighted bound. While the joint‐modelling formulation is well motivated, the inference steps and reparameterization tricks follow standard VAE/IWAE practice without novel algorithmic innovation.  
• Need for stronger baselines when prior knowledge is limited.  In the “agnostic” missing‐model regime (UCI Table 1, PPCA and DLVM variants), the gains over simpler matrix‐factorization‐plus‐mask‐concatenation methods (Sportisse et al., 2020a) are modest, and sometimes the agnostic not‐MIWAE is even “led astray” by a learned but misspecified pφ(s|x). It would strengthen the paper to include baselines that combine DLVM decoders with generic mask‐predictors (e.g. random forests) or to show how well pφ(s|x) can be learned purely from data without any self‐masking assumptions.  
• Empirical focus on self‐masking leaves other MNAR patterns unexplored.  While self‐censoring and clipping cover a useful class of MNAR mechanisms, real datasets often exhibit more complex dependencies (e.g. instrument threshold rules, user fatigue in surveys). A broader variety of missing models—perhaps via the group‐theoretic invariances suggested in Appendix C—would illustrate the generality of the approach.  
• Discussion of identifiability and recoverability is brief.  The paper notes (Section 3.1) that “some general missing models may lead to inconsistent estimation” and references Mohan & Pearl (2021), Nabi et al. (2020), Sportisse et al. (2020b). However, it does not detail which architectural or distributional assumptions guarantee that pθ(x) is identifiable from pθ,φ(xo, s). A short theoretical remark or additional synthetic study on identifiability would help readers understand model limitations.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Extend comparisons to generic mask‐classifiers.  In addition to logistic self‐masking, consider evaluating pφ(s|x) implemented via decision trees or random forests when no prior sign constraints are available. This would show how robust not‐MIWAE is in the “agnostic” setting.  
2. Explore richer MNAR mechanisms.  Introduce synthetic MNAR processes where missingness depends on interactions of features (e.g. s depends on x1 · x2) or group‐invariant rules (e.g. translation‐invariant missing masks in images), as foreshadowed by Appendix C.  
3. Add a brief identifiability study.  On synthetic data, vary whether the missing‐model architecture is correctly specified, under‐specified, or over‐specified; track downstream bias in pθ(x) and demonstrate how misspecification of pφ(s|x) can break identifiability.  
4. Clarify architecture choices.  In the SVHN convolutional encoder/decoder (Appendix A.2, Tables A2–A3), explain why temperature 0.5 was chosen for Gumbel‐Softmax in the categorical decoder, and discuss any stability issues encountered during reparameterized sampling in pixel space.

Conclusion  
Overall, this manuscript makes a useful contribution by adapting deep latent variable models to the MNAR setting through a joint data‐and‐mask likelihood bound, and demonstrates empirical gains on both low‐dimensional benchmarks and a large‐scale recommender dataset. Although the methodological novelty is incremental over MIWAE and the experiments focus primarily on self‐masking scenarios, the work addresses an important gap in deep generative modelling with missing data. I recommend “6: Marginally above acceptance threshold.”

reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Review:

This paper handles the problem of missing not-at-random (MNAR) by extending the MIWAE model to MNAR scenarios. To do this, they use the reparametrization trick in the data space to get the stochastic gradients of the lower bound.

Minor questions/comments:

- At the beginning of section 2, $\mathbf{x}_i$ is defined as a row the data matrix. However, I have noticed that the $i$ subscript is dropped several times across the text and left simply as $\mathbf{x}$. Maybe a small comment indicating this at the beginning could help to not get confused while reading section 2.

- In section 4.4. the authors say that both a categorical and a Gaussian observation model are used. I was under the impression that this paper was only evaluated on real attributes, mainly based on the use of MSE and RMSE as evaluation metrics. Are the datasets in Table 1 also a mixture of discrete and continuous variables? In that case, how do the authors compute the MSE of the discrete variables in Table 3 and, if it applies, in Table 1?

Summary:

The problem of MNAR is very important in practical scenarios, specially when handling tabular data. The paper was well written and explained, and although it can be seen as a simple extension to the MIWAE model, nonetheless I consider it to be a relevant and interesting contribution.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
This paper tackles a long-standing and practically important challenge in the analysis of incomplete data—namely the case where the mechanism that produces missing values depends on the values themselves (MNAR). Building on the Missing-data Importance-Weighted Autoencoder (MIWAE) framework, the authors introduce the not-MIWAE, which jointly models the data-generating process and the missingness mechanism. A key technical innovation is the use of the reparameterization trick not only in latent space (as in standard VAEs and IWAEs) but also in the data space. This allows efficient stochastic-gradient optimization of a tight lower bound on the joint likelihood of observed values and missing-mask patterns, without resorting to sampling-based approximations or ignoring the missing mechanism altogether.

Strengths  
1. Clear Motivation and Formalism.  The paper starts by restating Rubin’s classic MCAR/MAR/MNAR taxonomy and emphasizes why MNAR renders the missing mechanism non-ignorable. The introduction of equation (2) and the graphical model in Figure 1a make crystal-clear why a joint model of pθ(x) and pφ(s|x) is required, and how conventional “ignore-and-impute” methods can lead to bias.  
2. Elegant Extension of MIWAE.  By extending the IWAE objective to include the missing data xm as part of the latent variables, the authors derive the not-MIWAE bound in equations (7–8).  They carefully show (in Section 3 and Appendix D) that the resulting estimator is indeed a valid lower bound that tightens as the number of importance samples K increases, inheriting the monotonicity and consistency properties of the IWAE.  
3. Reparameterization in Data Space.  The paper’s core methodological advance is to apply the reparameterization trick a second time, for pθ(xm|z).  This dual reparameterization enables low-variance gradient estimates for both the generative model parameters θ and for the missingness-model parameters φ, in a single end-to-end training loop.  The discussion around Figure 1b (the PPCA example) neatly illustrates how classical PPCA biased under MAR can be corrected when the MNAR mask is explicitly modeled.  
4. Flexible Missingness Modeling.  The missingness mechanism pφ(s|x) is parameterized by a neural network or logistic regression that can incorporate domain knowledge.  For example, “self-masking” (Section 3.1 and Table 1) uses a sigmoid on a linear mapping of xj to model censoring in continuous features, and the authors observe (Table A1) improved mask-prediction accuracy when the sign of the regression weight is known.  This design permits the user to inject prior information about the likely form of MNAR effects.  
5. Comprehensive Experiments.  The authors evaluate their method on (a) UCI tabular datasets with induced self-masking MNAR patterns, (b) the SVHN image dataset with synthetic pixel “clipping,” and (c) the real-world Yahoo! R3 music-recommendation benchmark in which training data are plausibly MNAR.  In all cases, not-MIWAE outperforms both the MAR-based MIWAE and a variety of baselines, including low-rank joint models (Sportisse et al. 2020a), missForest, MICE, propensity-based debiasing methods, and state-of-the-art collaborative-filtering architectures.  The gains are especially striking in SVHN—where not-MIWAE recovers brighter clipped pixels—and on Yahoo! R3, where the marginal distribution of imputed ratings more closely matches the held-out MCAR test set (Figure 4).  

Minor Questions / Comments  
- Section 2 Notation Clarification.  At the start of Section 2, xi is defined as the ith row of the data matrix X and is then sometimes written simply as x (dropping the subscript).  This is understandable once one is accustomed to latent-variable notation, but a brief parenthetical remark—“we drop the i subscript when discussing a generic random draw x∼p(x)”—would prevent confusion, especially in equations (2) and (3) where the integration over xm is over the same index.  
- Discrete vs. Continuous Observations.  In Section 4.4, the authors mention using both a Gaussian and a categorical observation model on the Yahoo! data, and in the UCI experiments they report MSE/RMSE for all features.  It would be helpful to state explicitly whether the UCI datasets in Table 1 contain purely continuous attributes (and thus Gaussian likelihoods) or if any discrete features were one-hot encoded and treated as Bernoulli/categorical.  If discrete variables were present, how was the MSE computed—on numeric codes, or via expected values of the categorical distribution?  A short note on how MSE is calculated for non-Gaussian outputs would improve reproducibility.  

Additional Observations  
- Section 3.1 briefly suggests that group-theoretic priors (e.g. equivariance to translations or rotations) could inform the missingness model.  A sentence or two outlining how equivariant architectures (Cohen et al. 2019; Bloem-Reddy & Teh 2020) might be integrated in πφ(x) would inspire readers interested in image or graph domains.  
- In Appendix B, the self-normalized importance sampling for imputation is cleanly derived.  A small empirical study comparing single-imputation (conditional mean) vs. multiple-imputation (resampled draws) could illustrate the gain in uncertainty quantification.  
- The PPCA example in Figure 1b is very instructive; extending this toy study by varying noise levels or threshold cutoffs would show the robustness of not-MIWAE even when the true θ,φ deviate from the linear PPCA assumptions.  

Summary  
This work provides a principled, practical, and easily extensible solution to deep generative modeling under MNAR.  By coupling a flexible missing-data network pφ(s|x) with an importance-weighted autoencoder and deploying the reparameterization trick in both latent and data spaces, the authors deliver superior likelihood estimates and imputations across a variety of tasks.  The manuscript is well written, the mathematics is sound, and the experiments convincingly demonstrate the value of modeling MNAR explicitly.  I strongly support acceptance.

reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper proposes an approach to training deep latent variable models on data that is missing not at random. To learn the parameters of deep latent variable models, the paper adopts importance-weighted variational inference techniques. Experiments on a variety of datasets show that the proposed approach is effective by explicitly modeling missing not at random data.

P1: The related work is well done. This paper reviews the most related studies in several lines of research, including missing data concepts and theories in statistics, missing not at random data in various applications, deep latent variable models for missing data, etc.

P2: The experimental results are quite extensive. This paper conducts experiments on a wide range of datasets from different domains: censoring on multi-variate datasets, clipping on image datasets, and bias on recommendation datasets. The paper also compares the proposed approach against a representative selection of state-of-the-art approaches.

C1: The main concern for this paper is the lack of technical depth. Using variational distribution to derive a tractable lower bound of of a joint likelihood function, using Monte Carlo estimates to unbiasedly approximate the lower bound, and using a reparameterization trick to obtain unbiased estimates of gradients of the lower bound are well-established techniques. It is important for this paper to highlight what is the novelty of the proposed approach in terms of technical innovation.

C2: This paper argues that the proposed approach allows for incorporating prior information about types of missingness. However, it is not clear to me what is the prior information and how does the proposed approach leverage the prior information. It is highly recommended for this paper to provide a formal formulation of the prior information in missing not at random data. It is also recommended for the paper to elaborate how the proposed approach uses the prior information and underlying motivation.

C3: The last sentence in the 4th page of this paper states that it is possible to show that a sequence of objective functions converges to the joint likelihood function. To make the statement more convincing, it would be better if the paper could include a proof that the sequence of objective functions theoretically converges.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper introduces the not-missing-at-random importance-weighted autoencoder (not-MIWAE), a deep latent variable model designed to handle missing data mechanisms that are dependent on the missing values themselves (MNAR). By augmenting a standard DLVM with an explicit missingness network pφ(s|x), and optimizing a lower bound on the joint likelihood of observed data and mask via importance-weighted variational inference (IWAE-style), the authors demonstrate that one can achieve better parameter estimation and imputation performance under MNAR settings. The main contributions include (1) the formulation of a joint DLVM + missingness model capable of encoding prior information about the MNAR mechanism, (2) the derivation of a tractable importance-weighted lower bound incorporating reparameterization in both latent and data spaces, and (3) extensive empirical validations on UCI tabular data, SVHN image clipping, and the Yahoo! R3 recommender-system dataset.

Strengths:
P1. Comprehensive Related Work. The manuscript provides a thorough survey of classical missing-data theory (Rubin, 1976; Little & Rubin, 2002), recent MNAR methodologies (Tang & Ju, 2018; Sportisse et al., 2020a,b), and the growing literature on DLVMs for incomplete data under MAR/MCAR (Nazabal et al., 2020; Ma et al., 2018; Mattei & Frellsen, 2019). Section 2 situates the proposed not-MIWAE clearly within this spectrum, acknowledging key predecessors such as MIWAE (Mattei & Frellsen, 2019) and highlighting how the new model generalizes IWAE (Burda et al., 2016) to non-ignorable missingness. This breadth of citation and contextualization is valuable.

P2. Extensive Empirical Evaluation. The authors evaluate not-MIWAE on three distinct settings: (i) controlled self-masking MNAR on UCI datasets (Banknote, Concrete, White Wine, Red Wine, Yeast, Breast Cancer), (ii) synthetic clipping in SVHN images, and (iii) real-world selection bias in the Yahoo! R3 dataset. Comparisons include linear PPCA variants, MIWAE, low-rank joint models, missForest, MICE, propensity-based debiasing techniques (MF-IPS, MF-DR-JL), and MNAR-aware methods (MF-MNAR, Logit-vd). In all cases, leveraging correct or partially specified missingness models (e.g. self-mask known sign) yields consistent improvements in RMSE and test log-likelihood. The diversity of domains strengthens the case that not-MIWAE is broadly applicable to MNAR problems.

Weaknesses:
C1. Limited Technical Novelty / Depth. The key technical components—the use of an importance-weighted lower bound, Monte Carlo estimation for unbiased objective approximation, and reparameterization tricks in both latent and data spaces—are all well-established. Algorithm 1 essentially follows IWAE with an added Bernoulli mask network. The paper would benefit from a clearer articulation of the novel aspects: for example, emphasizing the particular challenges of reparameterizing through pθ(xm|z) when modeling MNAR, or detailing the stability and variance properties of the bound when jointly optimizing θ and φ. As presented, the theoretical section reads more like a straightforward adaptation of MIWAE than a fundamentally new inference algorithm.

C2. Unclear Formalization of “Prior Information.” The authors claim that their framework “allows for incorporating prior information about the type of missingness (e.g. self-censoring)” (sec. 1.1), but the mechanism for encoding such information remains informal. In Section 3.1, self-masking is instantiated via πφ,j(x) = σ(a xj + b), but the choice of features, constraints on (a, b), or any Bayesian priors over φ are not rigorously specified. It would strengthen the manuscript to introduce a formal definition of “prior knowledge” (e.g. known sign of a, known clipping threshold b), show how to impose these constraints (hard via sign regularization or soft via priors in q(φ)), and discuss identifiability conditions under which the full data distribution can be recovered (c.f. Mohan & Pearl, 2021). Without this clarity, the claim that the model “breaks away from MAR/MCAR assumptions” remains largely qualitative.

C3. Missing Theoretical Guarantees. On page 4 the authors note that “under the moment conditions of Domke & Sheldon (2018, Theorem 3) … the sequence LK(θ, φ, γ) converges monotonically … to the likelihood.” Yet no proof sketch or formal statement appears in the main text, and the Appendix reference to these conditions is minimal. To increase confidence, the paper should include an explicit theorem statement (e.g. “Theorem 1: Under mild boundedness assumptions on the weights wki …”) and sketch the argument that extends IWAE convergence to the joint MNAR setting. As practitioners, we need assurances about when and how fast LK tracks the true log pθ,φ(xo, s). A brief proof or pointer to a fully worked-out derivation in Appendix D would be helpful.

Additional Comments and Suggestions:
– Section 1.1 introduces the not-MIWAE graphical model (Figure 1a). It would help readers to add a small worked example illustrating how pφ(s|x) is parameterized, for instance contrasting an agnostic MLP missing model versus a constrained logistic self-mask.  
– In Section 4.2, the trade-off between data-decoder flexibility and missing-model flexibility is noted: “in a parsimonious data model a very general missing process can be used …” A more systematic ablation—varying decoder depth or bottleneck dimension—could make this statement more precise.  
– For the SVHN clipping experiment, the use of W = −50 in Equation (11) is somewhat ad hoc. Please clarify how sensitive performance is to the steepness of the masking S-shaped logistic.  
– In Section 4.4, Yahoo! R3 results show strong gains over propensity-based methods without access to MCAR data for training. It would be beneficial to discuss potential failure modes: e.g. what if the assumed missingness network pφ(s|x) is badly misspecified?  
– To guide future work, Section 5 alludes to employing equivariant networks for πφ(x) (citing Cohen et al., 2019). A concrete proposal—e.g. using permutation-invariant layers over feature subsets when item-level missingness is exchangeable—would be a welcome addition.

In conclusion, while not-MIWAE is an interesting and practically useful extension of DLVMs to MNAR settings, the technical contribution is incremental, and key claims around prior-information encoding and theoretical convergence are under-supported. I recommend rejection in its current form. More rigorous treatment of the missingness prior, a formal convergence statement, and deeper technical novelty would be required for a future submission.

Rating: 4: Ok but not good enough – rejection

C1: The main concern for this paper is the lack of technical depth. Using variational distribution to derive a tractable lower bound of of a joint likelihood function, using Monte Carlo estimates to unbiasedly approximate the lower bound, and using a reparameterization trick to obtain unbiased estimates of gradients of the lower bound are well-established techniques. It is important for this paper to highlight what is the novelty of the proposed approach in terms of technical innovation.

C2: This paper argues that the proposed approach allows for incorporating prior information about types of missingness. However, it is not clear to me what is the prior information and how does the proposed approach leverage the prior information. It is highly recommended for this paper to provide a formal formulation of the prior information in missing not at random data. It is also recommended for the paper to elaborate how the proposed approach uses the prior information and underlying motivation.

C3: The last sentence in the 4th page of this paper states that it is possible to show that a sequence of objective functions converges to the joint likelihood function. To make the statement more convincing, it would be better if the paper could include a proof that the sequence of objective functions theoretically converges.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.original_rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

title: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning by Discovering Intrinsic Options

reviews.rating: 8: Top 50% of accepted papers, clear accept
reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The paper develops a hierarchical reinforcement learning algorithm and analyzes its behaviour in four robotic manipulation and navigation tasks. The approach is based on a two-level hierarchy, *scheduler* at the top and *worker* at the bottom. This is similar to other approaches in the literature and the algorithm uses many ideas and elements from existing algorithms. However, these ideas and elements are combined in a novel and well-justified manner. The result is an algorithm that yields good results in a range of problems. The experiments are well done. The paper is generally organised well and written clearly. Relevant literature is reviewed well. 

The paper can be improved by a more comprehensive and detailed analysis of the behaviour of the algorithm, in particular, the options that are found. Section 4.4 is useful but very short. It describes only two options. Perhaps such an analysis can be added to the appendix.

In the proposed algorithm, the scheduler outputs an option every K steps in the environment. It would be reasonable to question whether more flexibility would be useful here, one that allows for varying option durations.

The authors state in Section 2 that they 'will use the terms “goal”, “option”, and “skill” interchangeably.' In the literature, these terms refer to related but different concepts. Using them interchangeably is not good scientific practice.

I did not find Figure 1 particularly useful. The simple and intuitive structure of the algorithm does not come through in the figure.

Rating:
8: Top 50% of accepted papers, clear accept
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces HIDIO (HIerarchical RL by Discovering Intrinsic Options), a two‐level hierarchical reinforcement learning (hRL) framework that discovers a set of task‐agnostic, intrinsic options in a self‐supervised manner and then learns to schedule them to solve sparse‐reward robotic tasks. The scheduler πθ selects a latent option u every K steps, and the worker πφ executes primitive actions conditioned on u and the recent sub‐trajectory. Unlike previous approaches that ground options in predefined goal spaces or require hand‐crafted skills, HIDIO trains πφ with an intrinsic objective based on minimizing the conditional entropy of u given sub‐trajectories, together with a maximum‐entropy policy term (Eq. 5). The intrinsic discriminator qψ predicts u from short sub‐trajectories, encouraging diverse, identifiable behaviors. HIDIO is trained off‐policy with Soft Actor‐Critic (SAC) updates for both levels and is evaluated on four sparse‐reward environments (7-DOF REACHER, 7-DOF PUSHER, GOALTASK, KICKBALL), demonstrating higher success rates and sample efficiency compared to SAC, SAC+ActionRepeat, HIRO, and HiPPO.

Strengths  
– Novel combination of ideas. HIDIO synthesizes several existing components—latent‐variable options, entropy‐based intrinsic rewards (inspired by DIAYN/VIC), off‐policy SAC updates, and meta‐MDP construction—into a cohesive framework that does not prespecify option semantics or goal spaces.  
– Rigorous experimental validation. The authors tune each baseline (SAC, SAC+ActRepeat, HIRO, HiPPO) thoroughly (Appendix D), run three random seeds, and report success‐rate curves and AUC. HIDIO’s ablations cover six discriminator feature extractors (State, Action, StateDiff, StateAction, StateConcat, ActionConcat) and both “hard” and “soft” shortsighted discounting, showing that StateAction, Action, and StateDiff perform best (Fig. 3).  
– Clear evidence of option utility. Visualizations of frozen‐u rollouts (Figs. 5, 9) reveal low‐level skills—sweeping motions in PUSHER and directed navigation in KICKBALL—that are both diverse and reusable by the scheduler.  
– Strong writing and organization. Sections 3.1–3.3 progressively build from the sampling process (Eq. 2) to the scheduler objective (Eq. 3) to the worker’s intrinsic objective (Eq. 5). The background on options and the differences from VIC/DIAYN are concisely stated.  
– Public code release. The authors provide an ALF‐based implementation (https://github.com/jesbu1/hidio), facilitating reproducibility.

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Detailed option analysis. Section 4.4 illustrates only two option behaviors, but a deeper study would strengthen the paper. For example, measuring option coverage via sub‐trajectory clustering (as in Fig. 8’s velocity histograms) or computing pairwise divergences among options could quantify diversity. An extended appendix could present heat maps of option‐conditioned visitation counts, or ablations showing how option reuse frequency evolves over training.  
2. Fixed option duration. Currently the scheduler emits u every fixed K steps. Allowing variable option lengths—e.g., learning a termination function β(s, u) or using a Poisson‐distributed duration—could improve flexibility, especially in environments where some subgoals require fewer or more steps. A brief discussion or preliminary experiment on adaptive durations would be a valuable addition.  
3. Terminology consistency. Section 2 states that “goal,” “option,” and “skill” are used interchangeably, but in the options literature these terms have distinct definitions. Clarifying that in HIDIO “option” refers specifically to the latent vector u ∈ [–1,1]^D, while “skill” and “subgoal” are avoided or defined separately, would improve precision and avoid confusion.  
4. Figure 1 utility. The overall framework diagram (Fig. 1) adds visual clutter without clearly conveying the key sampling steps. A simplified flowchart—e.g., a two‐tier timeline showing scheduler decisions every K steps and intrinsic/extrinsic rewards—would make the algorithmic structure more immediately intuitive.

Minor Comments  
– In Section 3.2.2, the terms “hard” and “soft” shortsighted workers are introduced. A one‐sentence motivation for why η=1–1/K (“soft”) approximates a K‐step return could aid readers unfamiliar with RL discounting.  
– In the experiments, the action‐penalty coefficients differ between REACHER (0.0001) and PUSHER (0.001). A brief remark on how this choice affects exploration stability would be helpful.

Decision  
8: Top 50% of accepted papers, clear accept. The proposed HIDIO framework is well‐motivated, empirically strong across challenging sparse‐reward domains, and advances the field of self‐supervised option discovery in hierarchical RL. The suggestions above would further deepen the analysis but do not undermine the paper’s substantial contributions.

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper present HIDIO, a hierarchical RL method that leverages self-supervised losses to discover intrinsic options while learning a scheduler to leverage the learned options to optimize the accumulated reward. HIDIO differentiates from prior work through enabling the low-level network/worker to discover task-agnostic options that can be generalized to future tasks, thus requiring no pre-training of skills,  and at the same time makes minimal assumption about the task structure.

This paper is in general well-written with clarity. The proposed  method is technically sound and empirically outperforms existing methods. The experiments in four different tasks first demonstrate an ablation of different feature extractors used for the self-supervised loss and analyzed the each of their advantages/disadvantages; followed by experiments comparing HIDIO with existing hRL methods and show that HIDIO outperforms in all tasks. Although the experiments are conducted in two different domains and four different tasks, all four tasks seem to be similar in nature (i.e. all pushing and reaching tasks). It would be great to see how HIDIO compare with other methods in more complex task domains such as in the work of [1] and [2], where the skill discovery network need to work with high dimensional raw input data and computing state-similarity can be tricky.


[1] Lynch, C., Khansari, M., Xiao, T., Kumar, V., Tompson, J., Levine, S., & Sermanet, P. (2020, May). Learning latent plans from play. In Conference on Robot Learning (pp. 1113-1132).
[2]Chuck, C., Chockchowwat, S., & Niekum, S. (2020). Hypothesis-Driven Skill Discovery for Hierarchical Deep Reinforcement Learning. International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems, 2020.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
This paper presents HIDIO, a hierarchical reinforcement learning (hRL) framework that simultaneously (1) discovers task-agnostic, intrinsic options in a fully self-supervised fashion and (2) learns a high-level scheduler to deploy those options so as to maximize sparse, downstream reward.  Unlike many prior hRL approaches, HIDIO makes almost no assumptions about the task structure, requires no pre-training of motor primitives or goal-reaching skills, and instead lets a lower-level “worker” policy invent a set of latent, temporally extended behaviors purely by optimizing an intrinsic objective.  The upper-level “scheduler” then learns to switch between these discovered options every K steps to efficiently solve hard sparse-reward tasks.  Empirically, HIDIO attains higher success rates with fewer environment interactions than standard SAC, an action-repeat baseline, and two state-of-the-art hRL methods (HIRO, HiPPO) across four continuous control tasks (7-DOF PUSHER, 7-DOF REACHER, and two novel SOCIALROBOT navigation/manipulation tasks).

Strengths  
1.  Clarity and writing.  The paper is very well organized and clearly explains both the overall algorithmic framework and each modeling choice—especially the construction of the worker’s self-supervised objective via a discriminator that classifies options from sub-trajectories.  Figure 1 offers a concise visual summary of the scheduler–worker–discriminator interaction, and the notation remains consistent throughout.  
2.  Technical novelty.  HIDIO’s key contribution is treating latent options as variables to be discovered purely from sub-trajectory statistics, rather than grounding them in state-space goals as in HIRO or requiring pre-programmed skills.  This allows the same option set to be retrained on any new sparse-reward task by simply re-running the self-supervised discovery phase.  The design of the “meta-MDP”—in which each worker state carries the history within the current K-step window—is both elegant and essential for rendering the sub-trajectory discriminator loss Markovian.  
3.  Thorough ablations.  Section 4.1 evaluates six different feature extractors for the discriminator (e.g. State, Action, StateDiff, StateAction, etc.) and shows that combining action + next-state features (‘StateAction’) or using state differences (‘StateDiff’) consistently yields the best task performance and sample efficiency.  The paper also compares “hard” versus “soft” short-sighted discounting for the worker, concluding that both variants work similarly well but “hard” discounting can offer slightly more stable learning in some tasks.  
4.  Strong empirical results.  Across all four tasks the HIDIO variants (especially StateAction and Action-conditioned features) match or exceed the best competing methods.  Standard SAC and SAC+ActRepeat fail almost entirely on the two SOCIALROBOT tasks (GOALTASK, KICKBALL), whereas HIDIO solves them to 60–80% and 30–40% success rates, respectively.  Even on the more forgiving PUSHER and REACHER tasks, HIDIO reaches near-100% success in fewer samples than HIRO or HiPPO.  
5.  Insightful qualitative analysis.  In Section 4.4 the authors visualize example options discovered in the PUSHER and KICKBALL environments—showing that HIDIO learns low-level directional drives, sweeping arm motions, and obstacle-bypassing trajectories without any supervision.  This lends credence to the claim that the learned latent options capture meaningful motor primitives suitable for downstream scheduling.  

Weaknesses & Suggestions  
– Task diversity.  Although the four evaluated tasks span two simulated domains (a PR2 arm suite and a wheeled-robot SOCIALROBOT suite), all of them remain in relatively low-dimensional state spaces with engineered continuous features.  Push-and-reach as well as simple goal-navigation tasks are valuable benchmarks, but they do not fully stress-test HIDIO’s ability to handle high-dimensional raw inputs (e.g. images) or highly varied object arrangements.  It would strengthen the claims if HIDIO were also compared on more complex domains such as those in Lynch et al. (2020) (“Learning Latent Plans from Play,” image-based play data) or Chuck et al. (2020) (“Hypothesis-Driven Skill Discovery,” hierarchical skill discovery from objects in rich 3D scenes).  In those settings, computing useful sub-trajectory representations or state similarities can be more challenging, and it would be interesting to see how HIDIO’s discriminator-based intrinsic loss scales.  
– Pre-training vs. joint training.  While the paper briefly examines a pre-training ablation (Fig 6), further discussion could help clarify when joint discovery and scheduling is most important—especially in environments where early intrinsic options might later hinder exploration if kept fixed.  A more detailed analysis of the pre-training fraction ρ across tasks (beyond the two fractions tested) could offer guidance for practitioners.  
– Hyperparameter sensitivity.  HIDIO introduces several new hyperparameters (dimension D of latent u, option interval K, discriminator network size, β for entropy trade-off).  Although Section D lists the ranges searched, a more concise plot showing performance as K or D varies (beyond the Appendix) would aid reproducibility.  

Overall, this is a solid, well-engineered contribution to hierarchical RL.  By eliminating the need for hand-crafted goals or pre-programmed skills, HIDIO represents an important step toward more generally applicable option learning methods.  For these reasons, I recommend acceptance.  

References for suggested domains for future work:  
[1] Lynch, C., Khansari, M., Xiao, T., Kumar, V., Tompson, J., Levine, S. & Sermanet, P. (2020). Learning Latent Plans from Play. In Conference on Robot Learning.  
[2] Chuck, C., Chockchowwat, S. & Niekum, S. (2020). Hypothesis-Driven Skill Discovery for Hierarchical Deep Reinforcement Learning. In IROS.  

Rating: 7: Good paper, accept.
