Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.confidence: 5: The reviewer is absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct and very familiar with the relevant literature
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Summary
-------
This paper proposes a model that, given one graph, predicts a sequence of edits that transforms this graph to the next one in a sequence of evolving graphs. To this end, it proposes the graph edit network (GEN), which is a linear output layer that transforms node embeddings to a set of scores that are then used to deterministically select graph edits. The space of edits predictable by this model covers the full space of graph changes. The paper moreover shows that, given a graph matching and a pair of graphs, we can algorithmically find a near-optimal graph edit sequence for generating training data. Finally, the paper demonstrates the model's capabilities on a set of synthetic benchmarks.

Strong points
------------------

+ The paper is well written and properly embedded in the literature.
+ The approach of solving graph transformation by predicting a complete sequence of graph edits (i.e. including deletion) is novel and certainly very interesting.

Weak points
------------------

- I am not really convinced by the approach. There is no way for the proposed model to know if it is predicting step K+1 of the ongoing edit sequence or step 1 of a new sequence (put differently: the model has no memory/is stateless; unlike to e.g. existing recurrent graph generative models). There is also no signal telling the user that the edit sequence is finished. So it seems to me that neither the model nor the user can know which edit steps are actually predicted graphs and which are just intermediate states. 
- The experiments only use synthetic toy datasets and a single baseline each. There are no comparisons with recently published models using the common, but supposedly inferior method of generating the next graph from scratch.
- Theorem 1 does not "prove that graph edit networks can approximate the NP-hard graph edit distance" or show that "edits predicted by the GEN can be sharply bounded to be close to optimal", as (incorrectly) claimed in the introduction/abstract. The theorem merely shows that the space of possible edits is complete and that the edit sequence length of your training data can be bounded if you have an (NP-hard) optimal graph matching. There is no guarantee whatsoever on what the GEN might predict. Also, GEN doesn't use any mapping, so the statement "provided that an optimal mapping is used" is rather confusing.
- Theorem 1 merely transforms the graph edit problem into a graph matching problem. This is stated on page 5, but considering that graph matching is still NP-hard, the importance of Theorem 1 seems to be overstated throughout the rest of the paper. Especially since it is largely just a variant of Proposition 1 by Bougleux et al. 2017 (as cited).
- The hinge loss with margin is rather common in deep learning, see e.g. [1]. The novelty of the proposed loss function is thus significantly overstated. [Side note: Its description on page 6 also seems overly complicated. I would recommend that you simplify this.]
- The paper states that the model can achieve linear instead of quadratic runtime if the number of edge edits are limited. While this might be true, there is no measure on how such a constraint would affect model performance. The experiment described in the last paragraph of the experimental section only provides some data on scaling behavior, not on accuracy. Especially considering that this restriction limits the number of edits by the model and thus breaks its universality it can severely affect the outcome. [Side note: The number of non-negative edge filter scores actually does not need to be constant, it merely needs to scale with sqrt(N) instead of N. So you actually have more wiggle room than you allow yourself.]

Recommendation
--------------
I recommend rejection, mainly because of significantly lacking experiments and overstated theoretical results. The presented theoretical results are interesting but do not really give deep insights about proposed approach (specifically Theorem 1). The overall theoretical contribution is significantly smaller than the authors make them out to be.

Feedback for the authors
------------------------
I really appreciate the novel approach of predicting a complete instead of a limited edit sequence. I would recommend acceptance if you a) significantly extend the experiments to include real (ideally practically relevant) graph sequences and directly compare to multiple relevant methods, which you already mention in the related work, b) solve the stopping problem mentioned above (i.e. distinguishing between another and the first edit step), and c) make your statements more accurate and avoid overstating your results. Since especially points a) and b) are unlikely possible to be solved within 2 weeks I strongly encourage you to build on this work and submit a better version at the next conference.

[1] Junbo Zhao, Michael Mathieu, and Yann LeCun. Energy-based generative adversarial network. ICLR 2017


[Update after author responses]: The authors have addressed most of my concerns and I've updated the score accordingly.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 2: The reviewer is willing to defend the evaluation, but it is quite likely that the reviewer did not understand central parts of the paper
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The authors consider Graphical Neural Networks in the time series model: a sequence of "edits" (in its general form) describe the difference between two graphs. This approach generalizes over previous works where changes on node attributes or addition and deletion of lone edges were considered.
The advantages of this work are more pronounced in applications where the output of the neural network can be encoded as a number of graph edits (this number being much smaller than the size of the whole graph). This is well motivated.
The only weakness of this work is its technical novelty. I think all the ingredients of the work were already available. Though I like the combination as well as their theoretical analysis.
I also think the paper is relevant enough to be cited:
@inproceedings{li2019graph,
  title={Graph Matching Networks for Learning the Similarity of Graph Structured Objects},
  author={Li, Yujia and Gu, Chenjie and Dullien, Thomas and Vinyals, Oriol and Kohli, Pushmeet},
  booktitle={International Conference on Machine Learning},
  pages={3835--3845},
  year={2019}
}

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
This paper introduces a novel way to produce graph‐structured outputs from graph neural networks (GNNs) by generating a sequence of sparse “graph edits” rather than reconstructing an entire adjacency matrix or node feature set.  In each time step t, the model (called a Graph Edit Network, or GEN) computes node embeddings via a standard message‐passing GNN and then applies a lightweight linear output layer that predicts which nodes to delete, which new nodes to insert (and how to connect them), and which edges to flip.  This approach unifies and extends previous work on edge‐only dynamics (e.g. Goyal et al., 2020; Hajiramezanali et al., 2019) and node‐attribute time‐series (Yu et al., 2018) by permitting arbitrary graph‐to‐graph transformations, including changes in the node set itself.  

Strengths  
1.  Generality of graph edits.  By adopting the classic graph‐edit framework (Sanfeliu & Fu, 1983; Zhang & Shasha, 1989), the GEN covers all possible modifications—node deletions, insertions, relabelings, and edge flips—so it can model arbitrary dynamics such as growing networks, churn, or structural rewiring.  This overcomes limitations of dense decoders (e.g. Jin et al., 2019; You et al., 2018a,b; Zambon et al., 2019) that must re‐generate the entire graph from scratch, and of link‐prediction approaches (Liben‐Nowell & Kleinberg, 2007; Goyal et al., 2020) that hold the node set fixed.  

2.  Sparsity and interpretability.  Because real‐world graph changes tend to be local and sparse—only a few nodes or edges change at each step—the edit‐based output layer yields far fewer decisions than a full adjacency‐matrix decoder.  This not only speeds up inference (linear time in the number of changes rather than quadratic in the number of nodes) but also makes each prediction human‐interpretable: one can simply list the added and removed nodes/edges at each step.  

3.  Theoretical guarantees.  The authors give a rigorous analysis showing (a) how to turn any bijection between node sets into a multi‐step teaching signal for the GEN; (b) that this procedure introduces at most 2(C–1) extra edits (where C is the number of connected components in the target graph); and (c) that, if the underlying mapping is optimal, GEN’s predicted script is within 2(C–1) of the true graph‐edit distance.  This near‐optimality result extends and clarifies ideas from Riesen & Bunke (2009) and (Bougleux et al., 2017) in the context of neural decoding.  

4.  Proof‐of‐concept experiments.  On a suite of synthetic dynamic‐graph tasks—including a small “edit‐cycle” domain over 4‐node graphs, a “degree‐rules” system that grows and shrinks towards 4‐cliques, and even Conway’s Game of Life on a 10×10 grid—the GEN achieves near‐perfect precision and recall for node insertions/deletions and edge flips.  It also handles tree‐structure dynamics (Boolean‐formula simplification, Peano‐addition rewrites) with 100% accuracy, outperforming a Gaussian‐process–based kernel predictor (Paaßen et al., 2018).  These results convincingly demonstrate that the model can learn a wide variety of graph‐to‐graph mappings that are difficult for baselines.  

5.  Computational efficiency.  By filtering edge‐change candidates through two per‐node scores (e+ and e–), the GEN output layer runs in O(N + R^2) time (where R is the number of nodes marked for possible edge changes), rather than O(N^2).  Empirically, on a large co‐authorship graph (Leskovec et al., 2007), GEN with filtering shows nearly linear scaling in both forward and backward passes, making it practical for real graphs with thousands of nodes.  

Weakness  
•  Technical novelty.  The individual ingredients—GNNs for node embedding, graph‐edit‐distance theory, local binary classifiers—are all well established.  What is new here is the specific output‐layer design and its training/inference scheme, together with the tight theoretical analysis of edit‐script length.  While each component has appeared in prior work (e.g. Bai et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019 on approximating GED with neural nets; You et al., 2018; Jin et al., 2019 on graph generation), their combination into a practical GNN decoder that directly emits edit scripts and avoids pooling layers is a useful advance.  

•  Engineering complexity.  Although the GEN simplifies decoding to local decisions, handling node insertions (which require dynamically expanding the graph) and crafting multi‐step training signals adds implementation overhead.  In practice one must carefully construct node‐mapping approximations (e.g. via Hungarian proposals or heuristics) and ensure the network is expressive enough to separate edit‐classes.  The paper acknowledges this and leaves optimizations (e.g. joint training of the mapping) to future work.  

Relevance and Citations  
This work will interest anyone working on dynamic‐graph modeling, neural message‐passing architectures, or graph‐generation tasks where sparsity and interpretability are important.  It complements recent graph‐GAN and variational‐autoencoder approaches (You et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2017; Fu et al., 2020) by offering a sparse, local, edit‐based decoder.  In particular, it is relevant alongside “Graph Matching Networks for Learning the Similarity of Graph Structured Objects” (Li et al., ICML 2019), which uses GNNs to compare static graphs, and follow‐on work on approximate GED (Blumenthal et al., 2020).  

Summary  
Overall, the Graph Edit Network offers a well‐motivated, broadly applicable method for predicting graph‐structured outputs in a time‐series context.  Its combination of edit‐distance theory, sparse decoding, and GNN embedding strikes a good balance between generality, efficiency, and interpretability.  While the conceptual novelty is incremental—most components exist in isolation—the integrated design and supporting theory make it a solid contribution.  I therefore recommend acceptance (rating 7: good paper).

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper proposes an algorithm to learn dynamics on graphs: node scores are computed by a GNN and then used to predict the modification of the graph (node/edge insertion/deletion). 

The training of the GNN is unclear to me. In particular, the authors do not describe how they obtain the 'teaching signal'. Theorem 1 is of little help as 'graph mapping' are not defined.

I have a fundamental problem with this paper: it looks like the GNN is trained to mimic the teaching signal but as claimed by the authors on page 5 '...the key theoretical result of our paper yields a simple way to construct teaching signals...'. Hence what is the point of using a GNN if a simple algorithm gives a satisfactory answer?

I do not agree with the authors about the expressive power of GEN: for simplicity, consider a cycle graph, then for a GNN as the one described in (2), the node representation will all be the same. Hence the node scores will be the same for all nodes and the algorithm 1 will have the same output for all nodes and edges. What am I missing?

[After rebuttal]  Thanks for the clarification, the aim of the authors becomes clearer. However I still think the paper requires more work before publication. 
Your definition of graph mapping is still unclear. From your definition, it looks like the only dependence of $\Psi$ with respect to the graphs $G_t$ and $G_{t+1}$ are through their number of vertices N and M: " bijective mappingψ:{1,...,M+N} → {1,...,M+N}with  the  additional  restriction  that  for  the  setInsψ:={j≤N|ψ−1(j)> M}we  obtainψ−1(Insψ) ={M+ 1,...,M+|Insψ|}"
How can this mapping be related to the edit distance of $G_t$ and $G_{t+1}$.
After reading the other reviews, I think the authors should clarify if their gaph mapping is related to the standard graph matching pb see https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graph_matching.

Rating:
3: Clear rejection

Hence what is the point of using a GNN if a simple algorithm gives a satisfactory answer?

I do not agree with the authors about the expressive power of GEN: for simplicity, consider a cycle graph, then for a GNN as the one described in (2), the node representation will all be the same. Hence the node scores will be the same for all nodes and the algorithm 1 will have the same output for all nodes and edges. What am I missing?

[After rebuttal]  Thanks for the clarification, the aim of the authors becomes clearer. However I still think the paper requires more work before publication. 
Your definition of graph mapping is still unclear. From your definition, it looks like the only dependence of $\Psi$ with respect to the graphs $G_t$ and $G_{t+1}$ are through their number of vertices N and M: " bijective mappingψ:{1,...,M+N} → {1,...,M+N}with  the  additional  restriction  that  for  the  setInsψ:={j≤N|ψ−1(j)> M}we  obtainψ−1(Insψ) ={M+ 1,...,M+|Insψ|}"
How can this mapping be related to the edit distance of $G_t$ and $G_{t+1}$.
After reading the other reviews, I think the authors should clarify if their gaph mapping is related to the standard graph matching pb see https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graph_matching.

Rating:
3: Clear rejection

title: Sample-Efficient Automated Deep Reinforcement Learning

reviews.confidence: 5: The reviewer is absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct and very familiar with the relevant literature
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper tackles the well-known issue of hyperparameter sensitivity and sample inefficiency in deep reinforcement learning (RL). The authors introduce SEARL, a population-based automated RL framework that jointly optimizes hyperparameters and neural network architectures for any off-policy RL algorithm. The key innovation is to share collected experience across a population of agents via a shared replay buffer, drastically improving sample efficiency. In a case study on TD3 within MuJoCo continuous control environments, SEARL reduces the number of environment interactions by up to an order of magnitude compared to random search and a modified Population-Based Training (PBT) baseline. Additional experiments include a held-out comparison against tuned TD3 and a meta-optimization of Rainbow DQN on Atari benchmarks, further illustrating SEARL’s generality and robustness. The paper provides clear algorithmic descriptions (Algorithm 1 and Figure 1), extensive ablations (Figure 5), hyperparameter schedules (Figure 4), and an open-source implementation.

Pros:
+ Novel shared-experience component. By storing rollouts of all N population members in a single replay file, SEARL allows each agent to benefit from the diverse behaviors of its peers. This design departs from standard PBT, where each worker trains on its own buffer, and results in a reported 10× improvement in sample efficiency (Section 5, Figure 2).
+ Detailed methodological exposition. The authors provide a thorough breakdown of the evolutionary loop (initialization, evaluation, selection, mutation, training), clear pseudocode (Appendix B), and precise descriptions of mutation operators (Appendix A). This makes the framework easy to understand, reproduce, and extend.
+ Rigorous, fair evaluation protocol. The paper argues convincingly for counting all environment interactions used during meta-optimization, not just those of the best final run (Section 4.3). This protocol offers a transparent comparison among SEARL, random search, and PBT.
+ Strong empirical results. In five MuJoCo tasks (HalfCheetah, Walker2d, Ant, Hopper, Reacher), SEARL converges to high return with up to 10× fewer environment steps than baselines (Figure 2). Ablation studies confirm that shared replay is the primary driver of this gain, though both hyperparameter adaptation and architecture evolution contribute positively (Figure 5).
+ Demonstrated generality. Beyond TD3, the authors show that SEARL can tune Rainbow DQN on Atari games (Pong, Enduro, Boxing, Freeway, RoadRunner), obtaining comparable or superior performance with fewer interactions (Appendix J, Figure 10). A held-out experiment (Section 5, Appendix H, Figure 9) further underlines SEARL’s robust transfer across similar tasks.

Cons:
- Applicability limited to off-policy methods. The shared replay mechanism, which yields most of the efficiency gain, cannot be directly ported to on-policy algorithms. As the authors note, applying SEARL to on-policy RL would require redesigning the experience sharing, potentially diminishing the sample-efficiency benefits.
- Missing discussion of contemporaneous AutoML/AutoRL work. While the paper cites Zoph & Le (2016) and a “Self-Tuning Actor-Critic Algorithm” (Zahavy et al., 2020), it omits some relevant lines of research. For example, “Off-Policy Actor-Critic with Shared Experience Replay” by Schmitt et al. (2019) also shows dramatic sample-efficiency gains via replay sharing, albeit without hyperparameter search. A short comparison in Related Work would help situate SEARL more precisely.
- Architecture adaptation impact may be muted in MuJoCo. The ablation (Figure 5) suggests that neural-architecture mutations yield only marginal improvements in these continuous-control tasks. Since MuJoCo agents often use shallow MLPs, more complex domains (e.g., pixel-based control or hierarchical tasks) might better showcase SEARL’s online architecture search capability.
- Limited exploration of mutation schedules. The paper presents schedules for learning rates and network sizes (Figure 4), but it would be informative to see how architecture adaptations evolve in tasks with image observations, where deeper or convolutional networks are common. SEARL’s ability to grow convolutional layers dynamically remains underexplored.

Detailed Comments and Suggestions:
1. Shared Replay’s Core Role.  
   – Section 3 introduces the shared experience replay buffer as a central innovation. The ablation study in Section 5 (Figure 5) confirms that disabling shared replay severely degrades performance in all MuJoCo tasks, much more so than removing architecture mutations or learning-rate adaptation.  
   – This finding aligns with prior distributed-RL systems (Ape-X, IMPALA), but here the goal is meta-optimization rather than scaling. Emphasizing this distinction underscores SEARL’s unique contribution.

2. Fair Meta-Optimization Protocol.  
   – The authors’ proposal to count all environment interactions used during HPO (Section 4.3) is a key step toward reproducible and comparable meta-RL results.  
   – Future work could formalize these metrics, such as plotting “cumulative return vs. total interactions (training + tuning)” for wider community adoption.

3. Dynamic Configuration and NAS.  
   – In Figure 4, SEARL quickly expands the actor network from a single hidden layer of 128 units to over 1 000 units by 0.5 M steps in HalfCheetah, then plateaus as capacity suffices. Similar schedules for other environments (Appendix G) indicate SEARL’s ability to adapt architectural capacity to task complexity.  
   – However, in environments like Walker2d and Ant, the optimal architecture remained relatively small. A more challenging domain—e.g., visual navigation—could better reveal the advantage of evolving convolutional features.

4. Extensions to On-Policy RL.  
   – Although SEARL focuses on off-policy methods, the key idea of dynamic hyperparameter and architecture adaptation could be transferred. One possibility is to use an inter-worker experience pool for on-policy actors, or to combine SEARL with recent self-tuning gradient methods (Zahavy et al., 2020) for the differentiable subset of hyperparameters.

5. Related Work and Citations.  
   – The discussion in Section 2 could be expanded to briefly mention Schmitt et al. (2019)’s “Off-Policy Actor-Critic with Shared Experience Replay,” which empirically validates the shared replay concept outside of meta-search.  
   – Similarly, recent AutoML work on online hyperparameter schedules (e.g., Parker-Holder et al., 2020) might warrant a comparison.

6. Code and Reproducibility.  
   – The open-source release (github.com/automl/SEARL) is commendable. Providing Docker images or scripts to reproduce key plots (Figures 2, 5, 10) would further ease adoption.

Conclusion:
Overall, SEARL presents a significant advance in sample-efficient AutoRL for off-policy algorithms by combining evolutionary hyperparameter search, dynamic architecture evolution, and—critically—a shared replay buffer across the population. The empirical gains (up to 10× fewer interactions) are convincing in MuJoCo and Atari environments, and the framework is both well-described and reproducible. Despite some limitations—most notably its current restriction to off-policy methods and modest architecture gains in shallow domains—the approach offers a compelling direction for automated, dynamic RL optimization. I therefore recommend acceptance. Rating: 7 – Good paper, accept.

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Summary：
In this paper, the authors intend to propose an efficient automated reinforcement learning (RL) framework. To achieve this goal, they integrate three technologies, i.e., evolutionary RL for hyperparameter search, evolvable neural network for policy network design, and shared experience replay for improving data usage. The paper uses a case study on MuJoCo to demonstrate the claimed advantages over baselines.

Some pros:
1. The motivation is good. The automation of reinforcement learning is beneficial to the research community, especially for researchers who are not familiar with RL but in need of it.
2. The framework is general and friendly to users. This framework is general and can be regarded as a plug-in module for a series of reinforcement learning methods. Besides, as we all know, both of the autoML and RL have a heavy computational burden, the adaption of evolvable neural network and shared experience replay greatly alleviate this dilemma. 
3. The organization of this paper is easy to follow. We can follow the authors from why they want to deal with the problem, to how they are inspired by existing work, and then to how the algorithms are design based on the questions to be answered and the existing technologies. 

Some cons:
1. The experiment is far from enough. Actually, this paper only has a case study. First, the author claims that the framework can optimize arbitrary off-policy RL algorithms, why only try on TD3? From the perspective of robustness, the authors need to compare more off-policy algorithms with and without the proposed method. Second, the paper claims there is no directly comparable approach for efficient AutoRL, which I do not agree with. In its own related work, many AutoRL baselines are listed, e.g., H. L. Chiang et al.  'Learning navigation behaviors end-to-end with autorl', F. Runge et al. 'Learning to design RNA'. For these baselines, they can either take the same exploration steps and compare performance with the proposed method, or compare the performance/computational cost when reaching the same performance. Anyway, the readers expect to see more comparisons with more baselines from more perspectives. Third, the authors claim that to tackle the non-stationarity of the RL problem, existing studies can substantially increase the number of environment interactions, implying the proposed framework has advantages on non-stationarity RL environments, but still, no experimental results are given.
2. Some technical details are missing. The logic is clear, the solution is reasonable, but the details are ignored. It is a good idea to keep the writing compact, but the lack of details may harm the readability of the paper. For example, since this is a general framework, how should we design hyperparameter settings of SEARL in initialization for different algorithms? In the training part, why should individual be trained for as many steps as frames have been generated in the evaluation phase, and why the training time could be reduced by using only a fraction j of the steps?

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review:

Summary:
This paper presents SEARL, a Sample-Efficient Automated Reinforcement Learning framework that jointly optimizes off-policy RL agents and their hyperparameters (including network architecture) via an evolutionary loop combined with shared experience replay. SEARL maintains a population of agents that collect rollouts into a single replay buffer, then applies tournament-based selection, mutation operators (perturbing weights, activations, layer sizes, hyperparameters), and gradient-based training on all individuals. A case study on meta-optimizing the TD3 algorithm in five MuJoCo continuous control tasks (HalfCheetah, Walker2D, Ant, Hopper, Reacher) demonstrates up to a 10× reduction in environment interactions compared to random search and a modified Population-Based Training (PBT) baseline, despite starting from small initial networks and zero prior knowledge. The authors also propose a fair evaluation protocol for HPO in RL, counting all environment steps used during meta-optimization.

Pros:
1. Strong Motivation and Relevance. Automating RL hyperparameter tuning is indeed an important and widely recognized challenge, especially given the sensitivity of deep RL algorithms to hyperparameters (e.g., learning rates, network size, discount factor schedules) and the high interaction costs in real-world settings. By addressing sample efficiency, dynamic configuration, and architecture search in one unified framework, the paper tackles three core obstacles to deploying state-of-the-art RL out of the box.
2. General and User-Friendly Framework. SEARL’s design is algorithm-agnostic and can be “plugged in” to any off-policy RL learner. The paper highlights how evolvable neural networks, together with shared replay, alleviate computational and sample-efficiency bottlenecks. Furthermore, the authors provide pseudocode (Algorithm 1) and an open-source implementation, lowering the barrier for adoption by practitioners.
3. Clear Organization and Presentation. The paper is well structured: Section 1 motivates the problem and identifies three desiderata (sample-efficient HPO, dynamic configuration, architecture search); Section 2 situates SEARL relative to ERL, ApeX/IMPALA, NAS, AutoRL and PBT; Section 3 details the evolutionary loop and shared replay; Section 4 outlines the TD3 case study, baselines, and a fair evaluation protocol; Section 5 presents results, ablations, and a held-out generalization experiment; and Section 6 concludes with a convincing argument for SEARL’s impact on real-world RL. Each component is introduced with sufficient background to be accessible even to readers less familiar with evolutionary methods.

Cons:
1. Limited Experimental Scope. The paper presents a compelling MuJoCo TD3 case study but stops short of evaluating SEARL on other off-policy algorithms or settings:
   - Although the authors claim SEARL generalizes to “arbitrary off-policy RL algorithms,” experiments are restricted to TD3. A broader empirical evaluation—e.g., on DDPG, SAC (Haarnoja et al., 2018) or CQL (Kumar et al., 2020)—would strengthen the claim of generality and robustness. Even a short set of experiments optimizing DDPG on one or two MuJoCo tasks could demonstrate that SEARL is not specific to TD3’s update rules or twin critics.
   - The related work section mentions AutoRL baselines (e.g., Chiang et al. 2019; Runge et al. 2019; Faust et al. 2019), but there is no empirical comparison. For instance, Runge et al. joint hyperparameter-architecture search could be run for the same total environment interactions, or Chiang et al.’s navigation AutoRL could be compared on one continuous‐control task. This would give readers concrete evidence of SEARL’s advantages in practice.
   - The paper also cites techniques addressing RL non-stationarity (e.g., François-Lavet et al., 2015; Tokic, 2010) but offers no experiments in settings with explicitly non-stationary reward distributions or changing dynamics. A simple environment with reward shifts (e.g., modified MuJoCo mass/inertia mid-training) would illustrate SEARL’s dynamic hyperparameter scheduling benefits over static HPO.

2. Missing Technical Details and Ablations. While the high-level algorithm is clear, some design choices would benefit from further justification and ablation:
   - Initialization of Hyperparameters: SEARL initializes the population’s architecture manually (e.g., one-layer networks with 128 nodes) and learning rates at the Adam default (1e–3). It would help readers to understand how sensitive SEARL is to this choice in other domains: should one always start from a minimal network and allow growth via mutation, or are there cases where fixed larger networks yield faster convergence?
   - Fraction j of Training Steps: The paper trains each individual for j=0.5 times the number of new evaluation frames. This fraction governs the balance between exploration (collecting data) and exploitation (gradient updates), but the rationale for 0.5 is not given. An ablation over j∈{0.25,0.5,1.0} would clarify how robust the method is to this parameter and guide practitioners in tuning it.
   - Replay-Sampling Strategy: SEARL uses uniform sampling from the shared replay buffer, but off-policy algorithms sometimes benefit more from prioritized replay. It would be interesting to know whether prioritized sampling within SEARL (either shared or per-agent) further boosts performance, or whether uniform replay suffices given the evolutionary diversity.
   - Mutation Rates and Operator Probabilities: The paper states that new layers are added with probability 0.2 and nodes with 0.8, but no justification is provided for this split. A small study varying these probabilities (e.g., more aggressive architecture growth vs. more weight perturbations) would help assess the necessity of each mutation operator.  

Suggestions for Improvement:
– Extend experiments to at least one additional off-policy algorithm (e.g., SAC) on a subset of MuJoCo tasks, reporting both sample efficiency and final performance under the same total environment interaction budget.
– Incorporate one AutoRL baseline directly in the empirical comparison, using the same evaluation protocol (Section 4.3) to showcase SEARL’s relative gains.
– Provide an experiment in a non-stationary environment (e.g., reward scaling or dynamics changes after 500K frames) to validate the dynamic HPO claim.
– Add an ablation over the training fraction j and discuss guidelines for setting j in practice.
– Clarify in the appendix the recommended initialization ranges for different algorithm classes (e.g., actor-critic vs. value-based) to help future users deploy SEARL without extensive trial-and-error.

Decision:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

Overall, this paper offers an innovative, well-motivated, and clearly presented contribution to automated RL with strong initial results in MuJoCo. Addressing the above concerns would make the work significantly more robust and broadly convincing, but even in its current form it provides valuable insights and a practical open-source tool.

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Summary: This paper propose a population-based AutoRL framework for hyperparameter optimization of off-policy RL algorithms. The framework optimizes both the hyperparameters and also the neural architecture in a one-shot manner, e.g., search and train at the same time. A shared experience replay buffer is used across the population, which as demonstrated in the experiments, substantially increase the sample efficiency compared to PBT and random search. 

Strengths:
- The idea is simple and intuitively makes sense. By sharing the experiences across the population each experience sample gets re-used more often during the training hence the increase in sample efficiency.

Weakness:
- Only apply to off-policy RL. 
- Random search is not a very compelling baseline. PBT is not optimized for sample efficiency. It seems that the shared replay buffer can also be applied to PBT? What about differentiable HPO methods? Those are often shown to be much more sample efficient compared to evolution based approaches. 
- There is little discussions on what the search results look like, e.g., the learning rate schedule or the neural architecture found by the search method. How are they compared to the SOTA learning rate schedule or neural architecture? Or maybe not even SOTA just compare to some simple heuristics that gradually decrease the learning rate or increase the network size. It would be of limited value if the use of AutoRL can only achieve marginally better performance compared to those simple heuristics.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold

reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
# Summary

The authors in this paper propose to optimize the hyperparameters and also the neural architecture while simultaneously training the agent. They evaluate the proposed method with TD3 in the MuJoCo benchmark suite. 

Overall, the proposed method is well-motivated and well-written, and they provide enough experiment/implementation details to reproduce the results. More importantly, they provide the source code. 

# Strength

- Tuning architecture and hyperparameters with PTB for off-policy RL has not been studied before. Compared to baselines, the proposed method is much more sample-efficient.
- The evaluation on MuJoCo is comprehensive, especially the ablation study in Section 4.

# Weakness

- They only test on a single benchmark with one method, TD3. MuJoCo is arguably simple. Actually, the visual world in MuJoCo is quite limited, so the encoder of the RL agent does not have to be huge. It could be more convincing if the authors can test on another benchmark, e.g. ProcGen. 
- I think compared to computer vision tasks with huge neural networks, the search space for the architecture of RL models is much smaller, which can be observed in the ablation study. Without architecture adaption, there is no observable difference. It seems that combining the tuning of architecture and hyperparameters is not that useful.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review:

# Summary

This paper presents SEARL, a Sample-Efﬁcient Automated Reinforcement Learning framework that jointly optimizes both hyperparameters and neural network architecture of off-policy RL agents during training. The core idea is to maintain a population of RL agents—each with its own architecture and hyperparameters—and apply evolutionary operators (selection, mutation) while simultaneously sharing experience in a common replay buffer. By replaying transitions across the population, SEARL aims to drastically reduce the number of environment interactions required for meta-optimization. The authors conduct a case study with the TD3 algorithm on the MuJoCo continuous control suite, comparing against random search and a modified Population-Based Training (PBT) baseline. Their results show up to a 10× reduction in sample complexity for meta-optimization, while achieving equal or better final performance in four out of five MuJoCo tasks. An extensive ablation study examines the contributions of architecture adaptation, hyperparameter schedules, shared replay, and weight mutations.

# Strengths

1. **Novelty of Joint Architecture and Hyperparameter Adaptation.** While prior work such as PBT has explored online tuning of scalar hyperparameters, this is, to my knowledge, the first effort to integrate neural architecture search (e.g., layer additions, node growth) into a PBT-like framework for off-policy deep RL. The use of simple neuroevolution mutations along with gradient-based updates is both conceptually clean and broadly applicable.

2. **Sample-Efficiency via Shared Replay Memory.** The shared experience replay across the population is an elegant idea that directly addresses the primary cost in RL—environment interactions. By reusing transitions collected by every member of the population, SEARL amplifies the utility of each sample. The experimental results clearly demonstrate that this design choice leads to up to an order of magnitude fewer interactions needed to discover strong hyperparameter configurations compared to both random search and a modified PBT that does not share replay.

3. **Comprehensive Experiments and Ablations.** The authors provide a thorough empirical evaluation on five MuJoCo tasks (HalfCheetah, Walker2d, Ant, Hopper, Reacher). They compare against both random search and a PBT variant that is extended to mutate network architectures. The ablation study in Section 5 isolates the impact of each SEARL component—shared replay, architecture adaptation, hyperparameter schedules, weight mutations—on performance. This level of analysis lends confidence that each element meaningfully contributes to the sample-efficiency gains.

4. **Reproducibility and Openness.** The paper is clearly written, with precise algorithmic descriptions (including pseudocode in the appendix). The authors open-source their implementation on GitHub, providing a valuable resource for the community to extend and apply SEARL to other off-policy RL algorithms.

5. **Fair Evaluation Protocol.** The authors argue convincingly for counting all environment interactions—including those used in meta-optimization—when reporting learning curves. This perspective addresses a common blind spot in AutoRL work, where the cost of hyperparameter search is often omitted. The log–log plots in Figure 2 clearly highlight the sample-efficiency advantages of SEARL under this protocol.

# Weaknesses

1. **Single Benchmark and Algorithm.** All experiments focus on TD3 in MuJoCo tasks—domains with low-dimensional state spaces and relatively simple visual observations. While MuJoCo is a standard continuous-control benchmark, it does not test SEARL’s ability to handle higher-dimensional inputs or more complex tasks such as those in the ProcGen suite [Cobbe et al., 2020] or pixel-based robotics domains [Haarnoja et al., 2018]. The limited scope makes it difficult to judge whether SEARL’s architecture mutations or hyperparameter schedules will generalize to vision-based or procedurally generated environments.

2. **Marginal Impact of Architecture Mutation.** The ablation study (Figure 5) shows that disabling architecture adaptation has little effect on final performance in several tasks. For instance, in Walker2d and Hopper, the “no architecture adaptation” curve almost overlaps with the full SEARL curve. This suggests that—at least within the relatively narrow search space of 1–3 layers and 64–500 hidden units that the authors consider—dynamic network growth may be less critical than hyperparameter tuning or shared replay. It raises the question of whether the added complexity of evolving architectures is justified in these simple continuous-control settings.

3. **Restricted Search Space.** The architecture search is constrained to adding at most one layer at a time or a fixed set of node increments (16, 32, 64). While this choice simplifies the implementation, the resulting search space remains quite small compared to NAS work in computer vision. A more compelling demonstration would show SEARL automatically discovering nontrivial architectures—e.g., deeper networks, varying layer widths, or alternative activation patterns—in response to task complexity.

4. **Lack of Vision-Based Evaluation.** Since many real-world RL applications involve raw image inputs, it would strengthen the paper to include at least one vision-based benchmark. For example, tuning DDPG or SAC on pixel-based MuJoCo observations, or applying SEARL to an Atari domain, could demonstrate its adaptability to convolutional neural network architectures and reveal whether shared replay helps in high-dimensional input spaces.

5. **Computational Overhead and Synchronization.** While SEARL is more sample-efficient than PBT, it requires synchronous evolutionary generations and periodic architecture re-initialization (including target network re-creation and optimizer state resets). The paper comments that this synchronicity prevents fully asynchronous scaling, which may become a bottleneck for very large populations or wall-clock time–sensitive applications. Further discussion of the tradeoffs between sample-efficiency and compute-efficiency would be helpful for practitioners considering SEARL.

# Suggestions for Improvement

- Evaluate SEARL on at least one vision-based task—either Atari or pixel-based MuJoCo—to assess the generality of architecture mutations and shared replay in high-dimensional input spaces.

- Extend the architecture search space (e.g., allow channel count or convolutional kernel search in vision settings) to better showcase dynamic adaptation capabilities.

- Provide wall-clock time comparisons (in addition to environment steps) against random search and PBT to clarify compute vs. sample tradeoffs.

- Investigate whether a wider range of architecture mutations (e.g., layer removal or layer duplication) yields further improvements, especially on more complex tasks.

- Discuss the potential of SEARL for on-policy algorithms and the adjustments required to replicate shared replay benefits.

# Rating

5: Marginally below acceptance threshold

While SEARL is a well-executed and sample-efficient AutoRL framework with several novel elements—particularly the integration of online architecture search into a PBT-style loop and the shared replay buffer—the paper’s empirical scope is limited to a single algorithm (TD3) on low-dimensional MuJoCo tasks. The modest impact of architecture adaptation in the ablation study and the absence of vision-based evaluations suggest that further validation is needed before recommending acceptance.

title: Contemplating Real-World Object Classification

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Real-world applications frequently provide challenges that are not seen in common computer vision datasets like Imagenet, where images are blurry, dark, corrupted, objects are highly occluded, test objects may be out of distribution due to natural distribution shifts, etc.  This phenomenon was investigated in 2018 by Beery et al, who similarly found categorization is easier with localization in challenging real-world scenes (ie classifying cropped boxes). I would recommend taking a look at that paper (citation below) and including it in the related work.

Beery, S., Van Horn, G., & Perona, P. (2018). Recognition in terra incognita. In Proceedings of the European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV) (pp. 456-473).


Pros:

The authors provide many experiments digging into various aspects of what makes real-world object detection challenging. This is a useful reference point for future work.

Cons:
This paper re-analyzes generalization in the context of an existing dataset. There is not anything particularly novel about the analysis, and similar results have been shown on other real-world datasets.
The authors describe the image distortions they consider to be “natural”, but are applying them synthetically. It is not clear to me that applying a synthetic distortion of a type that can be seen in the real world is necessarily reflective of those realistic distortions in the wild.  It would be better to explicitly collect examples of these types of distortions in real data and compare against that.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Below is a more detailed review that preserves all of the original critique points, elaborates on them with additional context, and offers suggestions for strengthening the paper—while retaining the same “marginally above acceptance threshold” recommendation.

Review:

This manuscript takes a close second look at the recently introduced ObjectNet dataset (Barbu et al., 2019) and investigates why state-of-the-art ImageNet‐trained CNNs suffer a dramatic accuracy drop when evaluated on realistic, in‐the-wild scenes.  The authors reannotate 18,574 images in the 113 categories shared between ObjectNet and ImageNet, draw object bounding boxes by hand, and repeatedly demonstrate that isolating the object (cropping to the box or segmentation mask) recovers 10–30% of the lost accuracy and substantially improves robustness to geometric transformations, noise corruptions, and adversarial attacks.  They also include a qualitative appraisal of extremely hard or mislabeled examples and show a close human–machine correspondence in which “easy” (or “hard”) samples for the network tend also to be easy (or hard) for human viewers.  Overall, the paper offers a comprehensive empirical atlas of where and why modern CNNs break on realistic data, and it provides a useful testbed and baseline for future work on generalization in object recognition.

Pros:
•  Thorough empirical exploration.  The manuscript reports experiments on six different popular CNN architectures (AlexNet, VGG-19, GoogLeNet, ResNet-152, Inception-v3, MNASNet) under a suite of synthetic perturbations (14 common corruptions at three severity levels), two white-box adversarial methods (FGSM and PGD-5), and three geometric shifts (rotation, scale, translation).  Having all of these numbers side by side—both on full scenes and on tight crops—gives a clear, quantitative picture of how much clutter and background bias contribute to poor real-world performance.
•  Insightful focus on context.  By annotating bounding boxes and comparing full‐image vs. box vs. segmentation performance, the authors rekindle the classic scene/context debate (Bar, 2004; Torralba & Sinha, 2001) and connect it directly to robustness.  The proposed “segmentation mask + foreground‐only” scheme is shown to be a surprisingly effective (though not yet practical) upper bound on robustness, suggesting a promising defense direction.
•  Qualitative analysis.  The paper does not stop at aggregate numbers; it highlights glaring annotation errors (e.g., mislabeled pillows or trays), shows cases that are intrinsically hard even for humans (e.g., dark, occluded, or motion-blurred objects), and argues convincingly that some categories in ObjectNet differ so strongly from their ImageNet counterparts that no data augmentation could trivially bridge the gap.
•  Useful baseline code and annotations.  The publicly released bounding‐box annotations and evaluation scripts will help the community replicate and extend the results, and they lay the groundwork for further studies on segmentation‐aware recognition.

Cons:
•  Limited novelty beyond reanalysis.  The core contribution is a careful reprofiling of Barbu et al.’s ObjectNet results—they show that cropping alone recovers a nontrivial fraction of the drop in accuracy.  While this is an important empirical finding, the paper does not introduce fundamentally new algorithms or theoretical insights.  Similar observations about the value of localization were made by Beery et al. (ECCV 2018, “Recognition in Terra Incognita”), who also found that cropping to detected animal bounding boxes significantly improved robustness across deserts, forests, and other unseen domains.  It would strengthen this manuscript to more explicitly compare to or build upon that work.
•  Synthetic “natural” distortions vs. real‐world corruptions.  The authors label Gaussian noise, frost, and motion blur as “natural,” but in the experiments these effects are applied synthetically to the ObjectNet images.  It remains unclear whether those synthetic corruptions faithfully capture the kinds of lighting changes, sensor artifacts, and motion distortions that occur in truly wild data.  A more compelling demonstration would come from collecting or curating a small set of real photos that exhibit each distortion type naturally (e.g., lab photographs taken under fog lamps, or motion‐blur frames from handheld videos) and showing that the same bounding‐box cropping yields similar gains.
•  Reliance on ImageNet pretraining.  All CNNs are initialized with ImageNet weights and then evaluated “as is” on ObjectNet.  The observed drop may partly reflect a domain mismatch in textures, viewpoints, and camera types, rather than an inherent limitation of CNNs.  Fine-tuning on out‐of‐distribution samples (if permitted) or on synthetic crops might further clarify whether the gap is due to missing background statistics or to more fundamental representation failures.
•  Absence of multi‐object or multi‐label evaluation.  ObjectNet scenes often contain several objects, yet the paper focuses exclusively on the single annotated label and top-1/top-5 metrics.  As noted by Russakovsky et al. (2015) and Shankar et al. (2020), multi‐label accuracy can better capture the presence of multiple items in a scene.  Annotating additional objects in each frame or reporting a multi‐label recall would give a fuller picture of scene complexity.
•  Human baseline restricted to presence judgments.  The original ObjectNet paper (Barbu et al., 2019) measured human accuracy at naming all objects in a scene (∼95% recall), but did not test rapid categorization under time pressure, nor did it collect detailed reaction‐time or confidence data for the boxed stimuli.  Reporting a direct human vs. CNN comparison on the cropped boxes, with time-limited trials, would help quantify the human–machine gap more precisely.

Suggestions for Improvement:
1.  Add Beery et al. (2018) to the related work and discuss how that field study on animal detection and classification in novel habitats dovetails with the current bounding-box results.  If possible, run a similar experiment on their “terra incognita” benchmarks to confirm generality.
2.  To address the synthetic vs. real distortion concern, compile a small side dataset of real‐world foggy, snowy, or motion-blurred images—perhaps by scraping Flickr or mobile phone archives—and show that the bounding‐box advantage persists.
3.  Consider a brief fine-tuning experiment in which classifiers are retrained (or at least linear-probed) on cropped ObjectNet boxes to see whether much of the remaining 15–20% gap can be closed by a domain adaptation step.
4.  Where feasible, add multi‐label annotations for a subset of scenes, and report recall@k in addition to top-1/top-5.  This will help disentangle errors that stem from “correct but unlabeled” objects vs. true adversarial or clutter failures.
5.  Although a full psychophysical study may be out of scope, gathering response‐time data or confidence ratings from human subjects on the isolated crops would offer a stronger human baseline.

Despite these limitations, the manuscript constitutes a valuable reference for understanding the role of context bias in real-world object recognition.  The finding that simple cropping recovers up to 30% accuracy and that segmentation masks yield adversarial gains is likely to influence future work on segmentation–recognition feedback and robust model design.  For these reasons, I recommend acceptance at ICLR, albeit with the caveat that the contribution is primarily empirical.  A rating of 6 (“Marginally above acceptance threshold”) seems appropriate.

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper revisits ObjectNet dataset closely and found applying classifiers on object bounding box significantly reduces the gap between ImageNet and ObjectNet. The authors further investigates the robustness of CNNs against image perturbations and adversarial attacks, and found limiting the object area to their segmentation mask significantly improves model accuracy (and robustness). Qualitative evaluation is also performed over confident and less-confident / incorrect model predictions and find it correlates with human perception.

Pros:
- More analyses like this paper does would help bridge the gap between ML/CV model performance in staged datasets and real-world scenarios
- The experimentation conducted in this paper is comprehensive, accompanied with many in-depth inspection over a large dataset. The insights drawn from this paper would be invaluable for researchers working in this field.

Cons:
- My major concern with this paper (and the main factor of rating it clear rejection) is the experimental setup used in section 3.3. From authors they "selected images from ten classes of the ObjectNet dataset ... manually annotated the object of interest in each image". Then "Models were trained on 70 images per category".  (also from Figure 39 "In total we annotated 1K images across ten categories of the ObjectNet dataset."). If interpreted correctly, the models are trained on part of ObjectNet images which clearly violates dataset license "ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model." (https://objectnet.dev/download.html). 

- While appreciating the authors conducting study on model robustness, the conclusion drawn from several experiments seems to confuse "performance" v.s. "robustness", where the former indicates model have better accuracy, and the latter measures how model accuracy varies with increasing noise / perturbations. See more details below.

Some (minor) comments:

- Sec 3.1. 1) I share the concern from authors that "object detector" should be not confused with "object recognition" (or commonly used "image classifier"). Hopefully vision community could use more consistent terms across literatures.

- Sec 3.1 1) While detection dataset would surely have more scale variation (and truncation / occlusion due to many objects are not in the center), it is not entirely clear that object in detection datasets "vary more in parameters such as lighting, ..., and blur".

- Sec 3.1 1) It would be great to see more analysis on detection datasets (authors mentioned they will discuss in section 4, but only with very little analysis).

- Sec 3.1, 2) While ImageNet and ObjectNet have distinct characteristics, having some stats on object size / spatial placement might better illustrate the gaps between these datasets.

- Sec 3.1 3) Agree top-5 might make classifiers' life easy, but it is more of an eval metric rather than training loss (the model still need to predict top-1 class correct during training). Meanwhile, it is not very clear why multi-label annotation would bias against model that "is spatially more precise but misses some objects". That model should be evaluated against detection benchmarks, rather than object recognition (image classification) datasets.

- Sec 3.1 "bounding box annotation". For multiple objects nearby with the same category, the annotation would include all of them in one bounding box. This might leads to bad aspect ratio? (in general, bounding box would also vary more in aspect ratio than images, and feeding bounding box into a square CNN seems to be less ideal)

- Sec 3.1 "object recognition results". "AlexNet, VGG-19, and GoogLeNet have also been used", GoogLeNet should be "ResNet-152"? ObjectNet uses inception (GoogLeNet) v4 while authors use v3.

- Sec 3.2.1 "The higher the prediction accuracy, the higher the robustness against natural distortions". This is not necessarily true. Looking at Figure 3, it seems all models and both image / bounding box schemes would have decayed performance w.r.t distortion severity, and their slopes seem similar.

- Sec 3.3 "Despite a large body of literature on whether and how much visual context benefits CNNs, the majority being focused on model accuracy, the matter has not been settled yet". "the matter" means context for robustness? please clarify.

- Sec 3.3 " Around 35.5% and 12.6% of the image pixels fall inside the object bounding box and the foreground object, respectively. Around 58.5% of the bounding box pixels fall inside the object mask.". here foreground object and object mask should be the same thing? if so, the numbers seem not matching (would expect box-to-image ratio * mask-to-box-ratio = mask-to-image ratio, or these numbers are normalized differently)?

- Figure 5: it is clear that seg-mask actually isn't robust to adversarial attacks (accuracy dropped significantly), which contradicts with the claim from authors.

- Sec 3.3 "An attempt was made to tune the parameters to attain the best test accuracy in each case", could authors elaborate? 

- Sec 3.3.1. here the results seem to indicate segmask is more robust (less variations). Would the segmentation mask itself be indicative for object categories? It might be interesting to predict object classes directly from those masks as a baseline.


Final review:

The authors updated the manuscript and removed tuning experiment on ObjectNet. I am still a bit concerned about the definition of "robustness", but the paper overall does look good for ICLR publication.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper undertakes an important reanalysis of the ObjectNet dataset (Barbu et al., 2019) by examining how restricting the input to object bounding boxes and segmentation masks affects both the raw accuracy and robustness of state-of-the-art CNN classifiers. The authors report that applying ImageNet-pretrained models to isolated object crops recovers roughly 10–15% of the performance loss originally attributed to ObjectNet relative to ImageNet, and that further limiting the input to segmentation masks yields consistent gains in robustness against common corruptions (Gaussian noise, blur, JPEG, etc.), geometric transformations, and adversarial attacks (FGSM, PGD-5). The paper complements these quantitative findings with a thorough qualitative analysis of “easy” versus “hard” ObjectNet samples, revealing that model confidence correlates well with human perceptual difficulty.

Rating: 6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

——

Pros:
- The paper addresses a highly relevant gap between staged academic benchmarks (e.g., ImageNet) and in-the-wild object recognition performance, providing actionable insights that could help the community develop more robust models.
- The experimental design in Sections 3.1 and 3.2 is comprehensive: it spans six popular CNN architectures (AlexNet, VGG-19, GoogLeNet/Inception, ResNet-152, Inception-v3, MNASNet), evaluates across 113 overlapping categories, and systematically probes 14 natural distortions at multiple severity levels.
- The segmentation-mask experiments in Section 3.3 are particularly valuable, suggesting that explicit foreground detection may serve as a strong inductive bias against both natural and adversarial perturbations.
- The qualitative inspection (Section 3.4 and Appendix C–D) is meticulous and offers the community a concrete understanding of why ObjectNet remains challenging even for humans, highlighting annotation issues, unusual object appearances, and counterintuitive labels.

Cons:
- My major concern is with the setup of the Section 3.3 experiments, which, as written, appears to contravene the ObjectNet license restriction against tuning on test data. The authors state that they “selected images from ten classes of the ObjectNet dataset … manually annotated the object of interest in each image,” then trained models on 70 images per category (1K images total). If this interpretation is correct, models are effectively fine-tuned on ObjectNet test images, which the dataset explicitly prohibits. This methodological choice could invalidate the robustness claims unless the authors clarify that they used a strictly disjoint split or obtained special permission.
- The terminology “robustness” versus “performance” is sometimes conflated. For example, the manuscript claims that “higher prediction accuracy implies higher robustness” (Sec. 3.2.1), but robustness more precisely measures the rate of accuracy degradation under increasing perturbation. In Figure 3, all curves decay with similar slopes across bounding-box and full-image settings, suggesting that while the absolute accuracy is higher for boxes, the relative sensitivity to distortion severity is comparable. It would strengthen the paper to separate these two notions explicitly and perhaps report a robustness metric such as the area under the corruption curve (mean corruption error) rather than raw top-1 accuracy at each severity.
- Section 3.1’s proposal to leverage object detection datasets for recognition studies is appealing, but remains only a brief discussion. Empirical statistics—e.g., distribution of object scales, occlusion levels, or illumination variations in COCO versus ImageNet—would better illustrate why detection datasets can fill biases left by recognition datasets.
- The aspect-ratio distortion introduced by merging multiple nearby objects into a single box (Sec. 3.1 “bounding box annotation”) could itself affect classification accuracy. Feeding highly elongated or padded boxes into a square network input (224×224) may distort object appearance; this source of variability is not isolated in the current analysis.
- In Sec. 3.1(3), the critique of the top-5 metric is well taken, but the follow-up suggestion to adopt multi-label accuracy could be elaborated. In particular, it is not clear why a classifier that “is spatially precise but misses some objects” would fare poorly under multi-label evaluation. Such a scenario seems more relevant to detection than recognition, and the connection could be tightened by contrasting single-label, top-k, and multi-label metrics on a small example set.
- Minor dataset details: in the caption for Fig. 39 (Sec. 3.3), the foreground-to-image and box-to-image pixel ratios (35.5% vs 12.6%) do not multiply out to the box-to-foreground ratio (58.5%), suggesting a mismatch in definitions or normalization. A clearer statement of how these percentages were computed would help reproducibility.
- In Figure 5, the segmentation-mask results under adversarial FGSM/PGD attacks still show a sharp accuracy drop at higher perturbations. The text’s claim that “segmentation masks significantly improve adversarial robustness” should be tempered to note that while masks yield a higher absolute baseline, the slope of the decline remains steep. Perhaps plotting robustness curves on the same axes would make the comparison more transparent.
- The statement “An attempt was made to tune the parameters to attain the best test accuracy in each case” (Sec. 3.3) is too vague. Which hyperparameters were optimized (learning rate, weight decay, data augmentation)? Were early-stopping or cross-validation applied? Detailed experimental settings—number of epochs, batch size, optimizer—should be provided (perhaps in an appendix) to ensure that differences in standard accuracy do not drive robustness disparities.
- Sec. 3.3.1’s geometric transformation results (Fig. 6) are convincing, but the paper could go further by evaluating shape-only baselines: e.g., predicting from mask contours or a silhouette autoencoder. Such a baseline would isolate how much segmentation alone, without pixel intensities, contributes to invariance under rotation and scaling.

Additional Suggestions:
- A small human-subject study that measures object recognition accuracy under forced rapid presentation of isolated crops versus full scenes would provide a direct baseline to compare against the CNN results, complementing Barbu et al.’s crowd-worker annotations.
- Annotating a subset of ObjectNet for multi-label ground truth would enable the community to explore detection-style metrics on this dataset without violating the original classification license.
- The authors might consider releasing their corrected bounding-box annotations publicly (the provided link currently only contains code), which would be a valuable resource for subsequent robustness benchmarks.

In summary, this paper surfaces important insights into how restricting the input to the object region can substantially mitigate performance drops on ObjectNet and bolster robustness to a variety of perturbations. The experiments are generally well thought-out and the quantitative gains are consistent across multiple architectures and distortion types. However, the potential license violation in the Section 3.3 fine-tuning experiments and some conflation between accuracy and robustness metrics must be addressed before publication. Assuming the authors clarify these methodological points and provide more thorough hyperparameter details, I believe this work makes a meaningful contribution and is suitable for ICLR.

6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
