title: Variational Structured Attention Networks for Dense Pixel-Wise Prediction

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper proposes a novel structure attention mechanism with a probabilistic CRF-like inference framework for the pixel-wise prediction, which not only models the spatial-wise dependencies but also considers the channel-level dependencies using an attention tensor. This attention mechanism is obviously different from the existing attention mechanism. Additionally, many quantitative and qualitative experimental results on three pixel-wise prediction tasks have demonstrated the superiority of the proposed method. The proposed attention mechanism is useful and flexible, which could be integrated into any pixel-wise prediction frameworks in theory. However, I think there exist some disadvantages:

1. There are some unclear statements in the paper. For instance, the authors say “the attention tensor is nothing but the sum of T rank-1 tensors”, but how are these rank-1 tensors generated by the spatial attention map and the channel-wise attention vector? Is each rank-1 tensor associated with the special regions of different objects? If the authors visualize the attention map, it would be better to understand. Moreover, the overall architecture of the proposed method is not clear. The authors do not specify the details of the multi-scale features, including the dimensions of them, where they come from, etc. The dimensions of the most important variables are also not specified, which makes the paper is hard to follow.

2. In the ablation study, when the model is ‘no structure’ on a single scale, does it denote the model does not integrate any attention mechanism? If so, when only considering the spatial or channel attention, why does it outperform DANet that includes dual attentions? If not, I think the comparison is not fair. 

3. Although the performance of the proposed attention mechanism outperforms most existing methods, the authors do not report the runtimes and parameters. Moreover, whether does it significantly burden the complexity of the model with the increase of T?

4. There are some grammar mistakes and typos. For example, “Since the exact a posteriori distribution is not computationally tractable, ...”, “Again, our method not outperforms …”

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper presents a novel structured attention mechanism wrapped inside a variational CRF-style inference framework, coined VISTA-Net, for dense pixel-wise prediction. The core idea, to decompose a full attention tensor of size W × H × C into a sum of T rank-1 components (each the tensor product of a spatial map m_t and a channel vector v_t), is original and potentially powerful. The extensive experiments on six public datasets—NYU-v2, KITTI, Pascal-Context, Pascal-VOC2012, Cityscapes and ScanNet—show consistent gains over strong baselines and prior art, including FCN+MS-CRF, Dual Attention Network (DANet), SANet, AG-CRF and DORN. The authors also integrate the inference updates (Z-step, M-step, V-step, K-step) directly as differentiable modules, enabling end-to-end learning with a standard CNN backbone (ResNet-101 / HRNetV2-W48). In general, the paper is well motivated and addresses an important limitation of existing attention modules (separate spatial and channel attention) by jointly modeling both types in a structured, low-rank fashion, underpinned by a clear probabilistic formulation and variational approximation.

Strengths  
• Novelty of structured attention formulation. Casting the full attention tensor as the sum of T rank-1 factors links back to classic low-rank modeling, yet applying it in this context is new.  
• Probabilistic treatment. By embedding the attention variables and CRF kernels into a joint variational framework, the method goes beyond heuristic multiply-and-add attention blocks.  
• Empirical gains. Across all tasks and datasets, VISTA-Net improves mIoU by ∼0.5–1.0% on segmentation, and reduces depth errors significantly, sometimes beating DORN by over 10% relative.  
• End-to-end implementation. The authors show how each update rule can be mapped onto standard convolutional layers and point-wise ops. This “inference as network layers” design is elegant and practical.

However, I share the original reviewer’s concerns. In particular, the following disadvantages should be addressed before final acceptance:

1. Unclear statements in the manuscript  
   – The paper states “the attention tensor is nothing but the sum of T rank-1 tensors” (Eq. 1), yet it never clearly explains how the spatial maps m_t ∈ {0,1}^{W×H} and channel vectors v_t ∈ Δ^{C} (simplex) are actually generated and initialized. Are they randomly seeded and then refined by the M-step / V-step (Eqs. 9–10)?  
   – It would help to link the algebraic form in Eq. 1 with the actual network modules in Fig. 2 and Fig. 14. For example, how does the concatenation of f_e and f_r (line 1 of Alg. 1) feed into convolution layers to produce m_t and v_t? A mini-diagram or even a summary table of tensor dimensions at each stage (e.g. f_e: [B,C,H,W], L: [B,T,H,W], Ach: [B,C,1,1], Asp: [B,1,H,W]) would greatly increase clarity.  
   – The intuition behind each rank-1 component is not discussed. Is each (m_t ⊗ v_t) intended to focus on a particular semantic part (e.g. sky vs. road vs. car)? If so, a qualitative visualization of each m_t and corresponding slice of the attention tensor (see Fig. 11 in the Appendix) for several images would deepen the reader’s understanding.  

2. Fairness and clarity in the ablation study  
   – In the ablation (Fig. 5 left), the “no structure – single scale” variant appears to mean “no attention at all,” but this isn’t explicitly stated.  Does it use the same CRF refinement (message passing) but with a fixed attention of ones?  If so, please clarify.  
   – When only spatial or only channel attention is enabled (but still in the variational CRF), these settings outperform DANet (which also has dual modules). This surprising result raises the question: are all methods compared with the same backbone, learning rate, number of iterations (T), and number of parameters? A side-by-side table of parameter counts and training schedules for these variants would help ensure apples-to-apples comparison.  

3. Runtime and model complexity  
   – The main text omits any discussion of inference time, GPU memory footprint, or parameter count.  Since the decomposition rank T can vary (e.g. T=1…9 in Fig. 5 right), how does this affect speed and peak memory?  Table 6 in the Appendix partially addresses this (FPS drops from 1.106 to 0.868 as T grows), but it should be summarized in the main paper as well.  
   – Modern front ends (ResNet-101 vs. HRNet-W48) have different FLOPs and latencies.  How significant is the added cost of the attention-CRF layers relative to the backbone?  A plot of mIoU vs. GFLOPs or mIoU vs. FPS would be informative for practitioners.  

4. Grammar mistakes and typos  
   – The manuscript contains a few ungrammatical phrases that hinder readability (e.g. “Since the exact a posteriori distribution is not computationally tractable, …”; “Again, our method not outperforms …”).  A thorough proofreading is needed.  
   – In Section 2.2, the phrase “kill two birds with one stone” appears; consider a more formal wording (e.g. “achieve two objectives simultaneously”).  
   – Several symbols appear inconsistently (e.g. φ_z vs. φz, ke,r vs. k_e,r) and should follow a unified notation.

Additional suggestions  
– Provide a concise pseudo-code box in the main paper, summarizing the inference updates as in Algorithm 1 of the Appendix, but trimmed to two pages total.  
– In Fig. 4 and Fig. 7, include failure cases (e.g. extreme lighting, thin structures) and discuss where VISTA-Net still struggles.  
– Discuss potential extensions, such as dynamic choice of T per image or per layer.  

Overall, this work makes an important contribution by unifying spatial and channel attention within a structured, low-rank, probabilistic framework. The performance gains are consistent and across diverse tasks. Once the above clarifications and additional empirical details are incorporated, the paper will be a strong candidate for acceptance.

Rating: 6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Review:
This paper proposes a novel structure attention mechanism with a probabilistic CRF-like inference framework for the pixel-wise prediction, which not only models the spatial-wise dependencies but also considers the channel-level dependencies using an attention tensor. This attention mechanism is obviously different from the existing attention mechanism. Additionally, many quantitative and qualitative experimental results on three pixel-wise prediction tasks have demonstrated the superiority of the proposed method. The proposed attention mechanism is useful and flexible, which could be integrated into any pixel-wise prediction frameworks in theory. However, I think there exist some disadvantages:

1. There are some unclear statements in the paper. For instance, the authors say “the attention tensor is nothing but the sum of T rank-1 tensors”, but how are these rank-1 tensors generated by the spatial attention map and the channel-wise attention vector? Is each rank-1 tensor associated with the special regions of different objects? If the authors visualize the attention map, it would be better to understand. Moreover, the overall architecture of the proposed method is not clear. The authors do not specify the details of the multi-scale features, including the dimensions of them, where they come from, etc. The dimensions of the most important variables are also not specified, which makes the paper is hard to follow.

2. In the ablation study, when the model is ‘no structure’ on a single scale, does it denote the model does not integrate any attention mechanism? If so, when only considering the spatial or channel attention, why does it outperform DANet that includes dual attentions? If not, I think the comparison is not fair. 

3. Although the performance of the proposed attention mechanism outperforms most existing methods, the authors do not report the runtimes and parameters. Moreover, whether does it significantly burden the complexity of the model with the increase of T?

4. There are some grammar mistakes and typos. For example, “Since the exact a posteriori distribution is not computationally tractable, ...”, “Again, our method not outperforms …”

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Overview

This paper carefully designs a structured attention network incorporating with a variational solution, to benefit the dense pixel-wise prediction via inferring the latent attention gate between spatial and channel-level features. Based on the suppose of T rank-1 attention tensors, the proposed structured attention network performs a CRF formulation with latent gating variables. 

Strengths

1.	Experiments are conducted on various dataset KITTI and NYU-v2 for depth estimation, PASCAL and Cityscapes for semantic segmentation, ScanNet for surface normal prediction. All the results demonstrate the outperformance.

2.	The motivation is clear. Jointly estimating spatial attention maps and channel attention vectors with a probabilistic framework.

Weakness

1.	The contribution is limited. Just a combination of two existing works, e.g., CRF-based models for multi-scale attention estimation and DANet for spatial and channel-wise attention. Why the T rank attention tensors are advanced than full-rank ones? How to guarantee the T channels optimal than the possible C?

2.	The generation of structured latent attention gate is confused. Compared with the work (Xu et al., 2017a), which illustrates the attention-gated CRFs with a clear graph, Figure 1 confuses me. Where is the gate? How to produce a probabilistically enhanced feature map? 

3.	The model is complex with four sets of latent variables (Z, M, V, and K) to be inferred. It is hard to be reproduced.

Questions:

1.	How to choose the parameter T? Ablation study might be added to demonstrate the difference with different T.

2.	In the inference stage, there are Z-step, M-step, V-step, and K-step. How to deal with so many variables to obtain the optimal performance? Is there any dependence on data size?

3.	For experiments of depth estimation, why the proposed method obtains optimal metrics but rel?

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review:

Summary  
This paper introduces VISTA-Net, a Variational Structured Attention network designed to enhance dense pixel-wise prediction by integrating spatial and channel attention within a probabilistic CRF framework. The core idea is to represent the 3D attention tensor a ∈ ℝW×H×C as a sum of T rank-1 components, each given by the outer product of a spatial attention map mt ∈ {0,1}W×H and a channel attention vector vt ∈ [0,1]C (Eq.1). The authors then formulate inference as learning four sets of latent variables—Z (hidden feature maps), M (spatial gates), V (channel gates), and K (CRF kernels)—via a variational approximation (Eqs.3–5). They implement iterative Z-, M-, V-, and K-steps as differentiable network layers, enabling end-to-end training of both the probabilistic model and the CNN backbone. Extensive experiments on six large-scale benchmarks (NYU-v2, KITTI for monocular depth; Pascal-Context, Pascal VOC2012, Cityscapes for semantic segmentation; ScanNet for surface normals) show that VISTA-Net outperforms strong baselines such as DANet, SANet, and AG-CRF.

Strengths  
1. Comprehensive empirical evaluation. The authors evaluate VISTA-Net across multiple tasks—continuous depth estimation, surface normal prediction, discrete semantic segmentation—and on six standard datasets. The breadth of experiments convincingly demonstrates the generality and competitiveness of the proposed model. For example, on KITTI monocular depth (Table 1), VISTA-Net achieves the best AbsRel = 0.063 and top accuracy δ<1.25, and on Pascal-Context segmentation (Table 2) it improves mIoU by about 0.6 points over the strongest AG-CRF baseline.  
2. Clear motivation for structured probabilistic attention. The paper clearly argues that unifying spatial and channel attention under a low-rank tensor decomposition (Fig. 1d) can capture richer inter-dependencies than separate or unstructured attention modules. Embedding this structure into a CRF-like model with variational inference further provides a clear probabilistic interpretation of the attention gates.  
3. Joint learning of CRF parameters and CNN backbone. By expressing the variational update steps (Z-, M-, V-, K-steps) as differentiable operations, the method allows the structured attention and CRF kernels to be learned alongside the convolutional feature extractor in an end-to-end fashion. This tight integration is a notable engineering contribution that simplifies optimization.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited methodological novelty. The core idea amounts to combining two existing paradigms—CRF-based latent gating from Xu et al. (2017a) and spatial/channel attention as in DANet (Fu et al., 2019)—with a low-rank tensor decomposition. While the variational formulation is carefully derived, the conceptual leap relative to prior work is incremental. In particular, it remains unclear why restricting the attention tensor to rank T < C (Eq. 1) is strictly superior to using full-rank attention maps. The authors claim that selecting T channels among C is “optimal,” but no theoretical guarantee or intuition is provided.  
2. Unclear generation of the latent attention gate. Compared to Xu et al. (2017a), which presented a clear graphical model for attention-gated CRFs, the manuscript’s Fig. 1 and related text leave the actual gate variable and its connection to the “probabilistically enhanced feature map” somewhat opaque. The precise mechanism by which the product mt⊗vt “gates” the message passing needs to be illustrated more explicitly, preferably with a factor graph or block diagram.  
3. Model complexity and reproducibility concerns. The approach introduces four intertwined latent variable sets (Z, M, V, K) and implements four update steps per iteration. This results in a fairly elaborate inference network whose hyperparameters (e.g. weight bp,c, number of iterations, initialization of M/V) and implementation details are nontrivial. Without public code or an in-depth pseudo-code, it may be hard for practitioners to reproduce the results or adapt the method to other backbones or tasks.

Questions for the authors  
1. How is the rank parameter T chosen in practice? Although an ablation study in Sec. 3.2 (and Fig. 5) shows performance for T up to 9, guidance on setting T for new datasets or budgets would be helpful. Does performance plateau or degrade for very large T?  
2. The variational inference comprises Z-, M-, V-, and K-steps. Can the authors clarify how the dependencies among these steps affect convergence and runtime? For instance, is the number of inference iterations fixed or dynamically determined per minibatch? Does the data size or feature map resolution significantly impact stability?  
3. In the depth estimation experiments, VISTA-Net achieves top performance on most metrics except, apparently, the mean relative error (rel). Could the authors comment on why rel behaves differently and whether any post-processing or normalization influences this measure?

Recommendation  
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

Overall, VISTA-Net presents a well-engineered integration of structured low-rank attention with variational CRF inference and demonstrates consistent empirical gains across diverse pixel-wise tasks. However, the methodological novelty is incremental over existing attention and CRF approaches, and critical details about gating construction and inference procedures should be clarified to facilitate reproducibility. Given the strong experimental validation and the clear articulation of a structured-probabilistic attention framework, I assign a score of 6 (marginally above acceptance threshold).

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper proposes a unified method to combine spatial and channel attention in a probabilistic framework, so that spatial and channel attention weights and probabilistic variables can be jointly optimized. The proposed method is incoporated in the proposed VISTA-Net to achieve state of the art performance in two dense pixel-wise prediction tasks: monocular depth prediction, semantic segmentation.

Overall this work is well motivated and organized in a good shape, so that I am on the positive side. I have some concerns as listed below.

1. My main concern is that this paper combines ideas of (Fu et al., 2019) and (Xu etal., 2017a), i.e., combining spatial and channel attention from (Fu et al., 2019) and Attention-Gated CRFs from (Xu etal., 2017a). 

2. In Eq.1, the attention tensor is limited to T rank. However, it is not clear to me how this can faciliate the inference in Eq.9.

3. In Eq.2, this paper proposes to additionally model CRF kernels. However, it is not explained why this is necessary. This is important as it is listed as a difference to existing method (Xu etal., 2017a). 

4. The approximation used in Eq.3 needs more details or reference to support.


Minor issues.
1. There are a lot of symbols in section 2. It would be better to draw a figure to include these symbols, while illustrating how the CRF formulation is incorporated in the attention mechanism.
2. The joint learning in section 2.2 can be formed in a algorithm procedure, so that others could see it more clearly.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
##########################################################################

Summary:

This paper proposes the VarIational STructured Attention networks (VISTA-Net), which improves pervious SOTA models for dense pixel-wise prediction tasks. The proposed VISTA-Net is featured by two aspects: 1) A new structured attention is proposed, which is able to jointly model spatial-level and channel-level dependencies; 2) It incorporates the proposed structured attention with a CRF-like inference framework, which allows the probabilistic inference. Experimental studies are conducted on monocular depth estimation and semantic image segmentation, showing improved performances of VISTA-Net consistently.

##########################################################################

Reasons for score:

Overall, I vote for rejection. My major concerns lie in three aspects, as detailed in Cons below: 1) This work is highly similar to Xu et al. (2017a) in terms of both methods and presentations. The difference is not significant; 2) While the presentation mainly follows Xu et al. (2017a), it needs some improvement; 3) The experimental studies lack more detailed analysis on the proposed method.

##########################################################################

Pros: 

1. The work is well-motivated. The aim of the proposed method sounds natural to me.

2. I like the ablation studies. But they could be performed on at least one more dataset.

##########################################################################

Cons: 

1. This work is highly similar to Xu et al. (2017a) in terms of both methods and presentations. The difference is not significant.
Method-wise, as discussed in Related Work, the difference only lies in that VISTA-Net takes channel-level dependencies into consideration. First, this means that "Moreover, we integrate the estimation of the attention within a probabilistic framework" (quoted from abstract) is not a novel contribution. Second, considering channel-level dependencies in attention has limited novelty. As discussed in Related Work, multiple studies have explored several ways. In addition, a key step in the proposed method is Equation (1), where the tensor multiplication operator is not explained. In my understanding, it should be the outer product, or more generally, Kronecker product. Missing the clear definition of this operator hinders the clarity in describing the proposed method.
Presentation-wise, the similarity is even higher. The entire section 2 follows the exact organization of section 2 in Xu et al. (2017a). By comparing the equations and presentations, it's more convincing that the novelty of this work is quite limited.

2. While the presentation mainly follows Xu et al. (2017a), it needs some improvement. First, the same notations as in Xu et al. (2017a) are used. However, some key things are not well explained. For example, the set of hidden variables $z_s$ corresponding to $f_s$ comes out from nowhere. I have to resort to Xu et al. (2017a) to know why we need $z_s$. Second, as mentioned above, the key steps like Equation (1) lack clear explanations.

3. The experimental studies lack more detailed analysis on the proposed method. It would be more meaningful to visualize those attention maps/gates instead of dense prediction results.

##########################################################################

Questions during rebuttal period: 

Please address and clarify the cons above

##########################################################################

Comments after the rebuttal period:

Pros:

First, it is totally acceptable to follow the notations and organization of Xu et al. (2017a), as long as the statements are clear and self-contained. The original submission failed on providing key details. The authors have made revisions to address this concern. Thanks!

Second, I appreciate the extra experimental results and visualizations.

Cons:

However, the authors' responses do not fully address my concerns about the novelty, especially method-wise. I will raise my score to 5, but still recommend rejection.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
